cover.title: Public and Community Housing Management Act 1992
cover.title.former: Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Act 1992

para.text: The Parliamentary Counsel Office has made editorial and format changes to this version using the powers under subpart 2 of Part 3 of the Legislation Act 2019.
Note 4 at the end of this version provides a list of the amendments included in it.

cover.reprint-note.admin-office: This Act is administered by the Ministry of Housing and Urban Development and the Ministry of Social Development.

history.history-note: Act name: replaced, on 1 October 2019, by section 31 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).

para.text: Title

history.history-note: Title: repealed, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).

prov.heading: Title and commencement

para.text: This Act is the Public and Community Housing Management Act 1992.
Except as provided in subsections (3) and (4), this Act shall come into force on the date on which it receives the Royal assent.
Section 38 shall be deemed to have come into force on 31 March 1989.
Section 41 shall come into force on a date to be appointed by the Governor-General by Order in Council.
An order under this section is secondary legislation (see Part 3 of the Legislation Act 2019 for publication requirements).

legtable.summary: The following table is small in size and has 3 columns that are grouped under the heading Legislation Act 2019 requirements for secondary legislation made under this section.

entry.emphasis: Legislation Act 2019 requirements for secondary legislation made under this section

row.entry: PCO must publish it on the legislation website and notify it in the Gazette
The Minister must present it to the House of Representatives
It may be disallowed by the House of Representatives

entry.emphasis: This note is not part of the Act.

history.history-note: Section 1 heading: amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Section 1(1): replaced, on 1 October 2019, by section 31 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Section 1(4): section 41 brought into force, on 4 June 1999, by clause 2 of the Housing Restructuring Act Commencement Order 1999 (SR 1999/161).
Section 1(5): inserted, on 28 October 2021, by section 3 of the Secondary Legislation Act 2021 (2021 No 7).

prov.heading: Interpretation

para.text: In this Act, unless the context otherwise requires,—
additional resident, in relation to any social housing, means a person who—
is aged 16 years or over; and
is financially independent (as defined in Schedule 2 of the Social Security Act 2018); and
resides or is to reside in the housing; but
is not an applicable person
agency means the social housing agency or agencies appointed under section 100
agreement includes a deed, a contract, an agreement, an arrangement, and an understanding, whether oral or written, express or implied, and whether or not enforceable at law
applicable person, in relation to any social housing,—
means every person to whom the housing is or is to be let; and
includes every person who is the spouse or partner of such a person
appointed day means 17 November 2000
assessable assets has the meaning given to that term by the calculation mechanism
assessable income has the meaning given to that term by section 108
authority means the authority appointed under section 159 to perform the functions and exercise the powers of the regulatory authority under Part 10
calculate includes ascertain
calculation mechanism means—
sections 107 to 113; and
the regulations for the time being in force under section 114
code of conduct,—
in relation to information that may be required under section 80, means the code of conduct issued under section 86:
in relation to information that may be required under section 125, means the code of conduct issued under section 138

community housing means any premises that are let or to be let by or on behalf of a registered community housing provider for occupation by any person as a place of residence (whether or not the premises are owned by the provider or any other person)
community housing provider means a housing provider (other than Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities) that has, as 1 of its objects, the provision of one or both of the following types of housing:
social rental housing:
affordable rental housing
contributions, in relation to an additional resident of any social housing, means all payments made by the resident, and the value of all goods or services provided or paid for by the resident,—
as a contribution towards the costs and expenses incurred by the applicable persons in residing there; or
in consideration of goods or services provided by the applicable persons while the resident is residing there; or
in consideration of the resident being allowed to reside there; or
for 2 or all of those reasons
income-related purpose has the same meaning as in Schedule 2 of the Social Security Act 2018
income-related rent,—
in relation to a tenant of Kāinga Ora housing, means a rent that the agency has—
calculated for the tenant under the calculation mechanism or Schedule 3; and
notified to Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities; or
in relation to a tenant of a registered community housing provider, means a rent that the agency has—
calculated for the tenant under the calculation mechanism; and
notified to the provider
joint Ministers means the Minister of Finance, the Minister of Housing, and the Minister for Social Development

Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities means Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities established under the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019, and includes any subsidiary of Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities

Kāinga Ora housing means premises (whether owned by the Crown, Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities, or any other person) let or to be let by or on behalf of Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities for occupation by any person as a place of residence
liabilities includes—
liabilities and obligations under any Act or agreement; and
deposits and other debt securities within the meaning of the Financial Markets Conduct Act 2013; and
contingent liabilities
market rent,—

in relation to Kāinga Ora housing, means the rent for the time being determined by Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities or the Tenancy Tribunal (under the Residential Tenancies Act 1986) as the market rent for that housing; or

in relation to housing provided by a registered community housing provider, means the rent for the time being determined by that provider or the Tenancy Tribunal (under the Residential Tenancies Act 1986) as the market rent for that housing
notification means notification by the agency under sections 103 or 106
partner, in the phrase spouse or partner and in related contexts, means, in relation to any person (A), a person who is A's civil union partner or de facto partner
prospective tenant means a person—
who—
is not a person to whom any social housing is let or to be let; and
has applied to the agency (alone or together with some other person or people) for an assessment of the person's eligibility for social housing and—
whose eligibility for social housing has not yet been confirmed or declined or whose application for an assessment has not yet been withdrawn; or
whose eligibility for social housing has been confirmed but who has not yet been allocated any particular social housing by a social housing provider; or
who—
is already a person to whom social housing is let or to be let; but

has applied to the agency (alone or together with some other person or people) to become a tenant of some other social housing and has not yet had the application accepted or declined, or withdrawn it
registered community housing provider means a community housing provider that is registered by the authority under Part 10 as a community housing provider
reimbursement agreement means an agreement referred to in section 85(1) or an agreement under section 98(1)
rent period, in relation to any social housing, means a period in respect of which the tenant is required by the tenancy agreement to pay rent for the housing
rights includes powers, privileges, interests, licences, approvals, consents, benefits, and equities of any kind, whether actual, contingent, or prospective
social housing means Kāinga Ora housing or community housing
social housing provider means Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities or a registered community housing provider

standard tax means the amount of tax reckoned on a weekly basis that would be deductible in accordance with tax code M specified in schedule 5, part A, clause 4, table row 1 of the Tax Administration Act 1994

study grant means a basic grant or independent circumstances grant under regulations under section 645 of the Education and Training Act 2020 (or an allowance, award, bursary, grant, or scholarship that the agency considers to have been established under that section in place of a basic grant or independent circumstances grant)
tailored agreement means an agreement under section 137A
tenant,—
in relation to social housing in general,—
means any person or people to whom any social housing is let or to be let; and
includes a prospective tenant; and
in relation to any particular social housing, means the person or people to whom it is let or to be let.
In this Act, a reference to transfer, authorise, or grant includes entering into an agreement to transfer, authorise, or grant, as the case may be.

References in this Act to person include an agency and any person who is an officer or employee in the service of the Crown in a government department or public body (other than as an officer of a court, in his or her official capacity).

prov.heading: Purpose of this Part and Parts 8 to 10

para.text: The purpose of this Part and Parts 8 to 10 is to reform the provision of social housing by establishing a framework for a multiple-provider social housing market.
This Part and Parts 8 to 10—

enable the assessment of need and eligibility for social housing, and eligibility for an income-related rent, and associated functions (such as providing advice, managing applications for social housing, carrying out investigations, and referring and allocating prospective tenants and tenants to social housing providers), to be performed by 1 or more government agencies that are independent of the agency that provides state housing:
define the role of Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities in the provision of social housing in a multiple-provider environment:

facilitate the growth of the community housing sector by enabling the extension, on terms and conditions set by the joint Ministers, of an income-related rent subsidy to registered community housing providers and an income-related rent to their tenants:

provide flexibility for the social housing agency and social housing providers to enter into tailored agreements for the provision of social housing and any related services, subject to ministerial direction:

enable the appointment of a government agency as a regulatory authority for registered community housing providers, with associated objectives, functions, and powers to monitor and enforce compliance with regulatory standards:
enable the making of regulations that prescribe eligibility criteria and performance standards to be met by registered community housing providers:
create certain offences for failure to comply with information requirements issued to tenants and associated parties by relevant government agencies.
In this section, government agency means any department, departmental agency, or Crown entity.

history.history-note: Section 70: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).
Section 70(2)﻿(b): amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).

prov.heading: Interpretation of terms used in this Part

para.text: In this Part, unless the context otherwise requires,—
prospective tenant for Kāinga Ora housing means a person—
who—
is eligible to be allocated social housing; and
is not a person to whom any Kāinga Ora housing is let; and
has been referred or allocated to Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities to be allocated, assigned, or let Kāinga Ora housing; or
who—
is already a person to whom Kāinga Ora housing is let; but

has applied to Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities (alone or together with some other person or people) to become a tenant of some other Kāinga Ora housing and has not yet had the application accepted or declined, or withdrawn it
tenant,—
in relation to Kāinga Ora housing in general,—
means any person or people to whom any Kāinga Ora housing is let or to be let; and
includes a prospective tenant for Kāinga Ora housing; and
in relation to any particular Kāinga Ora housing, means the person or people to whom it is let or to be let.

prov.heading: Income-related rent

para.text: This subsection applies to Kāinga Ora housing and a tenant if—
an income-related rent has been calculated for the tenant under Part 9, the calculation mechanism, or under Schedule 3; and
the agency has notified under section 106 the income-related rent calculated for the tenant; and
that notification of the income-related rent has not been replaced by a notification from the agency to the effect that the tenant must pay market rent.

If subsection (1) applies to any Kāinga Ora housing and a tenant, the rent for the housing must be the income-related rent for the time being notified by the agency, subject to subsections (4) and (5).
If subsection (1) does not apply to any Kāinga Ora housing and a tenant, the rent for the housing must be its market rent for the time being.
The income-related rent for any Kāinga Ora housing must not exceed its market rent for the time being.

If satisfied that special circumstances justify its doing so, Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities may, in its absolute discretion, set for and accept from a tenant of any Kāinga Ora housing a rent lower than the rent otherwise required by subsection (2) or (3) to be paid for the housing by the tenant.
Subsections (2) to (5) are subject to section 73.

prov.heading: Changes in rent

para.text: This section applies to an existing tenant of Kāinga Ora housing if Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities—
is required by section 72 or 74 to change the rent for the housing; or
is empowered by section 72 to change the rent for the housing, and decides to do so.
Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities must, in accordance with section 136 of the Residential Tenancies Act 1986, give a tenant to whom subsection (1) applies written notice of the change in rent stating—
the date on which it takes effect; and
the matters referred to in paragraphs (j) and (k) of section 13A of that Act.
In the case of a reduction in rent, the notice has effect as a variation of the tenancy agreement for the housing whether or not the tenant signs it.
In the case of an increase in rent, the notice must comply with section 24 of the Residential Tenancies Act 1986, except—

subsections (1)﻿(d) to (h) and (1A) of that section do not apply to the increase in rent if Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities has received a notification from the agency under sections 106 and 118A(3) of this Act that the rent for the housing must be its market rent for the time being:
to the extent that section 74 of this Act provides otherwise, in any other case.

history.history-note: Section 73: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).
Section 73(1): amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).

Section 73(4): replaced, on 2 March 2026, by section 20 of the Social Assistance Legislation (Accommodation Supplement and Income-related Rent) Amendment Act 2025 (2025 No 27).

prov.heading: Increase in rent due to change in tenant's circumstances

para.text: If Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities receives a notification from the agency under section 106 of an increase in an income-related rent due to a change in the tenant’s circumstances or the circumstances of an applicable person in relation to the tenant,—

Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities must, subject to subsection (4), give the tenant written notice, in accordance with section 73(2), stating the date on which the increased income-related rent takes effect (the effective date); and
the effective date must be a date no earlier than 61 days after the date (as stated in the agency's notification) that the change in circumstances occurred; and
Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities must give the notice to the tenant at least 2 weeks before the effective date.
The income-related rent notified under subsection (1)﻿(a) for any Kāinga Ora housing must not exceed the market rent for the time being for that housing.
The agency may recover from the tenant as a debt due to the Crown, the difference (if any) between—

the higher income-related rent calculated by the agency for the tenant for the housing in respect of the period commencing on the date that is 61 days after the change in circumstances occurred and ending on the day before the effective date (capped at the market rent); and
the income-related rent applying to that period that the agency had calculated for the tenant for the housing and set out in its previous notification.

Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities must change the rent charged following receipt of a notification from the agency unless, in the opinion of Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities, it would not result in a material difference to the rent charged for the housing concerned.
Section 24(1)﻿(c) to (h) and 24(1A) of the Residential Tenancies Act 1986 do not apply to the increase in rent.
Amounts recoverable under subsection (3) are not rent in arrear for the purpose of the Residential Tenancies Act 1986.

Mary is a tenant of Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities and is paying an income-related rent to Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities. On 1 March, Mary returned to full-time employment but did not tell the agency of her change in circumstances.

Several months later, the agency becomes aware that Mary’s circumstances had changed on 1 March. The agency calculates an increased income-related rent for Mary, and notifies Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities accordingly. The increased income-related rent does not exceed the market rent for the housing.

On 1 October that same year, Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities notifies Mary that she must pay an increased income-related rent (in accordance with the agency’s notification) from the rent period beginning on 21 October (the effective date).
The agency is entitled to recover from Mary, as a debt due to the Crown, the difference between—

the higher income-related rent for the housing (as set out in the agency’s notification), in respect of the period commencing on 1 May (61 days after the change in circumstances) and ending on 20 October; and
the income-related rent applying to that period that the agency had calculated for the tenant for the housing (as set out in the agency's previous notification).

prov.heading: Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities may review placement

para.text: Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities may—
allocate a prospective tenant for Kāinga Ora housing to particular Kāinga Ora housing:
review the eligibility of a tenant to be or to continue to be allocated, assigned, or let particular Kāinga Ora housing:

require a tenant to transfer to different Kāinga Ora housing (being housing that is appropriate for the tenant’s housing needs) if Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities considers that the transfer is necessary or desirable for any reason.
Nothing in this section limits or affects—
a tenant’s rights under the Residential Tenancies Act 1986:
the rights of Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities as a landlord under the Residential Tenancies Act 1986, including its rights to terminate a tenancy in accordance with that Act:
the functions and powers of Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities under this Act or any other Act.

prov.heading: Limits on obligations of Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities

para.text: Nothing in this Act requires Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities to provide any housing or particular housing to a tenant referred or allocated to Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities by the agency.

prov.heading: Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities may seek information

para.text: For the purposes of a review under section 75 or an investigation under section 16 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019, Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities may request any person to—
answer questions; or
allow Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities to inspect any document or other written information; or
give Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities—
a copy of any document or other written information; or
a printout of any information stored digitally.

The person does not have to comply with the request but (for the purposes of section 24 of the Privacy Act 2020) this subsection authorises the person to make personal information available in response to the request.

history.history-note: Section 79: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).
Section 79 heading: amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Section 79(1): amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Section 79(1)﻿(b): amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Section 79(1)﻿(c): amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Section 79(2): amended, on 1 December 2020, by section 217 of the Privacy Act 2020 (2020 No 31).

prov.heading: Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities may require information for certain purposes

para.text: Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities may by written notice require information from any person for any 1 or more of the following purposes:
for the purpose of any investigation under section 16 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019:
for the purpose of detecting whether a person has committed or is committing an offence under section 82 or 83:
for the purpose of determining and detecting the cost of fraud under section 82 or 83:

for the purpose of ascertaining whether a person has failed or refused to answer fully, or has deliberately given a false or misleading answer, to any question asked under section 17(1)﻿(a) of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019.
Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities when requiring any information under subsection (1) must do so in accordance with the code of conduct.
Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities may in writing require any person to advise whether any information provided under subsection (1) is accurate.
A person from whom information is required under subsection (1) or advice is required under subsection (3) must comply with the requirement—
no later than 5 working days after the notice was given; and
in the manner specified in the notice, without charge to Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities.

This subsection authorises (for the purposes of section 24 of the Privacy Act 2020) any person who is required to provide information under subsection (1) or advice under subsection (3) to make personal information available in response to the requirement.
Subsection (1) does not—
require any person to provide any information or produce any document that would be privileged in a court of law:
require any person to provide any information or produce any document that is legally professionally privileged.
Subsection (6) does not apply to information—
that consists wholly or partly of, or relates wholly or partly to,—
the receipts, payments, income, expenditure, or financial transactions of a specified person (whether a lawyer, his or her client, or any other person); or

investment receipts (being receipts arising or accruing from any money lodged at any time with a lawyer for investment) of any person or persons (whether the lawyer, his or her client, or any other person or persons); and
is contained in, or comprises the whole or part of, any book, account, statement, or other record prepared by or kept in connection with a trust account of the lawyer.
If a person refuses to disclose any information or document on the ground that it is privileged under subsection (6)﻿(a) or (b),—

Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities or that person or any other person to whom the information or document relates may apply to a District Court Judge for an order determining whether or not the claim of privilege is valid; and
for the purposes of determining that application, the Judge may require the information or document to be produced to the court.
In this section,—

lawyer means a barrister or solicitor of the High Court, and includes a firm or an incorporated law firm (within the meaning of the Lawyers and Conveyancers Act 2006) in which the lawyer is, or is held out to be, a partner, director, or shareholder
trust account, in relation to a lawyer, has the same meaning as in section 6 of the Lawyers and Conveyancers Act 2006.

history.history-note: Section 80: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).
Section 80 heading: amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Section 80(1): amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Section 80(1)﻿(a): amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Section 80(1)﻿(d): amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Section 80(2): amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Section 80(3): amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Section 80(4)﻿(b): amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Section 80(5): amended, on 1 December 2020, by section 217 of the Privacy Act 2020 (2020 No 31).
Section 80(8)﻿(a): amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Heading: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).
Heading: amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).

prov.heading: Placement in Kāinga Ora housing

para.text: The matters to which Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities may have regard in doing any of the things stated in subsection (2) may include criteria that have, or are capable of having, the effect that tenants, people who are or might be applicable persons in relation to those tenants, and other people who are or might be residing in the housing concerned are treated differently on the basis of—

their marital status, disability or absence of disability, age, or family status (as the terms marital status, disability, age, and family status are defined in paragraphs (b), (h), (i), and (l) of section 21(1) of the Human Rights Act 1993); or
whether or not they are resident, or ordinarily resident, or permanently resident, or lawfully resident, in New Zealand; or
their incomes; or
their property; or
2 or more of those factors.

The things are any thing that Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities does in the course of allocating, assigning, and letting Kāinga Ora housing to tenants, and in administering and terminating tenancies, and include—
allocating, assigning, and letting, or continuing to let, Kāinga Ora housing to a tenant; and
reviewing the eligibility of a tenant to be or to continue to be allocated, assigned, or let particular Kāinga Ora housing; and
terminating a tenancy; and
reallocating or reassigning Kāinga Ora housing to a tenant; and
retaining the current allocation, assignment, or letting of particular Kāinga Ora housing to a tenant.
Nothing in this section affects the application of the New Zealand Bill of Rights Act 1990.

history.history-note: Section 81: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).
Section 81 heading: amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Section 81(1): amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Section 81(2): amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Section 81(2)﻿(a): amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Section 81(2)﻿(b): amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Section 81(2)﻿(d): amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Section 81(2)﻿(e): amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Heading: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: Offence not to provide information or to provide false or misleading information

para.text: A person who is required to provide information under section 80(1) commits an offence if the person—
fails or refuses to provide, without reasonable excuse, the information required:
provides false or misleading information in response to the requirement.
A person who is required to advise under section 80(3) whether information provided under section 80(1) is accurate commits an offence if the person—
fails or refuses to provide the advice without reasonable excuse:
provides false or misleading information in response to the requirement.
A person who commits an offence against this section is liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding $2,000.

history.history-note: Section 82: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: Offence to mislead Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities for certain purposes or results

para.text: A person commits an offence who, for the purpose described in subsection (2) or with the result described in subsection (3),—
makes any statement knowing it to be false in any material particular; or
deliberately does or says anything for the purpose of misleading or attempting to mislead Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities; or
when required to provide information under section 80, deliberately omits to do or say anything for the purpose of misleading or attempting to mislead Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities.
The purpose is—
for that person or another person to be or continue to be allocated, assigned, or let particular Kāinga Ora housing, or to be allocated, assigned, or let some other Kāinga Ora housing:
for that person or another person to receive or continue to receive home-related financial assistance under the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019.
The result is that person, or another person, whether or not entitled to it,—
is or continues to be allocated, assigned, or let particular Kāinga Ora housing:
is allocated, assigned, or let some other Kāinga Ora housing:
receives or continues to receive home-related financial assistance under the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019.
A person who commits an offence against this section is liable on conviction to imprisonment for a term not exceeding 12 months or a fine not exceeding $5,000, or both.

history.history-note: Section 83: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).
Section 83 heading: amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Section 83(1)﻿(b): amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Section 83(1)﻿(c): amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Section 83(2)﻿(a): amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Section 83(2)﻿(b): amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Section 83(3): amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Section 83(3)﻿(a): amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Section 83(3)﻿(b): amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Section 83(3)﻿(c): amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Heading: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: Delegation of powers under this Part

para.text: Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities may not delegate any power under this Part except to—
an employee of Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities; or
a person engaged by Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities under a contract for services providing for the person to exercise that power; or
the agency or chief executive of the agency.

An employee to whom a power has been delegated by Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities in accordance with subsection (1) may, with the consent of Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities, subdelegate that power to another employee of Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities.

The agency or the chief executive of the agency to whom a power has been delegated by Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities in accordance with subsection (1) may subdelegate that power to an employee of the agency.
If Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities delegates a power under this Part to a body corporate engaged by Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities under a contract for services,—
the body corporate cannot subdelegate the power except to a person who is an employee of the body corporate; and
an employee of the body corporate to whom the power is subdelegated cannot subdelegate it further.
Subsections (1) to (5) override section 130(1) of the Companies Act 1993.

history.history-note: Section 84: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).
Section 84(1): replaced, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Section 84(2): repealed, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Section 84(3): amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Section 84(4): amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Section 84(5): amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Heading: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: Reimbursement agreement with Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities

para.text: Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities must, if required by the Crown, enter into 1 or more agreements for the provision by Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities of housing and related services to persons who pay income-related rents rather than market rents for the housing, in return for the payment by the Crown of the difference between the amounts of market rents for the housing and the income-related rents charged.

However, subsection (1) does not apply to the extent (if any) that a tailored agreement covers the provision by Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities of housing and related services to persons who pay income-related rents rather than market rents for the housing. (See section 137A.)

history.history-note: Section 85: replaced, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Heading: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: Code of conduct applying to obtaining information required under section 80

para.text: Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities, in consultation with the Privacy Commissioner, must, within 3 months after the commencement of this section, issue a code of conduct that applies in respect of any requirement for information under section 80.
The code of conduct—
must include the matters specified in section 90; and
may include restrictions on obtaining—
specified classes of information; and
information from specified classes of persons or from persons in specified relationships; and
must specify procedures applying to the obtaining of information under section 80.
Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities may from time to time, in consultation with the Privacy Commissioner, amend the code of conduct, or revoke the code of conduct and issue a new code of conduct.

Nothing in the code of conduct may derogate from any code of practice issued by the Privacy Commissioner under section 32 of the Privacy Act 2020 that applies to the information required under section 80, and Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities, in consultation with the Privacy Commissioner, must amend the code of conduct to conform with any such code of practice. This subsection is affected by section 88.

As soon as practicable after issuing any code of conduct and any amendment to it under this section, Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities must arrange for it to be published on an Internet site that is publicly available at all reasonable times or published in a form that is otherwise accessible to the public.
The code of conduct that, before the commencement of this section, was issued under Part 5 is, on the commencement of this section, deemed to be issued under this section.

history.history-note: Section 86: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).
Section 86(1): amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Section 86(3): amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Section 86(4): amended, on 1 December 2020, by section 217 of the Privacy Act 2020 (2020 No 31).
Section 86(4): amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Section 86(5): amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).

prov.heading: Who must comply with code of conduct

para.text: The following persons must comply with the code of conduct when requiring information under section 80:
Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities and every employee of Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities:
the agency and every employee and the chief executive of the agency:
every person or body corporate engaged by Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities under a contract for services providing for the person to exercise the power to require such information:
every employee of a person or body corporate referred to in paragraph (d).

history.history-note: Section 87: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).
Section 87(a): replaced, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Section 87(b): repealed, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Section 87(d): amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).

prov.heading: Regulations authorising information to be obtained

para.text: The Governor-General may, on the advice of the joint Ministers given after consultation with the Privacy Commissioner, by Order in Council, make regulations authorising Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities to obtain pursuant to a requirement under section 80—
any specified class of information; or
information from any specified class of persons; or
information in any specified manner.

Subsection (1) applies despite the fact that the making of that requirement would otherwise be in breach of any code of practice issued by the Privacy Commissioner under section 32 of the Privacy Act 2020.
Regulations under this section are secondary legislation (see Part 3 of the Legislation Act 2019 for publication requirements).

legtable.summary: The following table is small in size and has 3 columns that are grouped under the heading Legislation Act 2019 requirements for secondary legislation made under this section.

entry.emphasis: Legislation Act 2019 requirements for secondary legislation made under this section

row.entry: PCO must publish it on the legislation website and notify it in the Gazette
The Minister must present it to the House of Representatives
It may be disallowed by the House of Representatives

entry.emphasis: This note is not part of the Act.

history.history-note: Section 88: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).
Section 88(1): amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Section 88(2): amended, on 1 December 2020, by section 217 of the Privacy Act 2020 (2020 No 31).
Section 88(3): inserted, on 28 October 2021, by section 3 of the Secondary Legislation Act 2021 (2021 No 7).

prov.heading: Complaints

para.text: Any person who is required to provide any information under section 80, or who is the subject of that information, may make a complaint to the Privacy Commissioner that the requirement breaches the code of conduct.
Parts 5 and 6 of the Privacy Act 2020 apply to any such complaint as if the code of conduct were a code of practice issued under section 32 of that Act.

history.history-note: Section 89: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).
Section 89(2): replaced, on 1 December 2020, by section 217 of the Privacy Act 2020 (2020 No 31).

prov.heading: Matters to be included in code of conduct

para.text: The code of conduct issued under section 86 must contain the following matters:

provisions requiring the information to be first sought, as the case may require, except where compliance with such provision would prejudice the maintenance of the law, from the applicant for or recipient of home-related financial assistance under the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 or the spouse or partner of the applicant or recipient:

provisions allowing a person referred to in paragraph (a) the time that is specified in the code to provide the information before Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities requires that information or confirmation of that information from another person or agency, except where compliance with such provision would prejudice the maintenance of the law:

a provision prohibiting a requirement under section 80 being made in respect of a person referred to in paragraph (a) to any other person (not being a financial institution, a lawyer, employer, or former employer of the person referred to in paragraph (a), and not being a department, departmental agency, Crown entity), unless there is reasonable cause to make a requirement under that section:

a provision prohibiting a requirement under section 80 being made to an employer in respect of any information that relates solely to the marital or relationship status of an employee or a former employee of that employer:

provisions otherwise restricting requirements under section 80 being made to an employer to information specified in the code relating to the employment and the address of an employee or a former employee of that employer.
In subsection (1)﻿(c), reasonable cause includes—
cause to suspect that the person—
has committed an offence under this Part; or
has obtained by fraud any home-related financial assistance under the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019:

the fact that the person has failed, within the time specified in the code, or has refused, to provide information in accordance with a requirement made to that person under a provision referred to in subsection (1)﻿(a).

history.history-note: Section 90: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).
Section 90(1)﻿(a): amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Section 90(1)﻿(b): amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Section 90(2)﻿(a)﻿(ii): amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Part 8: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: Interpretation of terms used in this Part

para.text: In this Part, unless the context otherwise requires,—
prospective tenant for community housing means a person—
who—
is eligible to be allocated community housing; and
is not a person to whom any community housing is let; and
has been referred or allocated to a registered community housing provider to be allocated, assigned, or let community housing; or
who—
is already a person to whom community housing is let; but

has applied to the registered community housing provider (alone or together with some other person or people) to become a tenant of some other community housing offered by that provider and has not yet had the application accepted or declined, or withdrawn it
tenant,—
in relation to community housing in general,—
means any person or people to whom any community housing is let or to be let; and
includes a prospective tenant for community housing; and
in relation to any particular community housing, means the person or people to whom it is let or to be let.

history.history-note: Section 91: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: Income-related rent

para.text: This subsection applies to community housing, a registered community housing provider, and a tenant if—
the tenant is an eligible tenant who—
has been allocated or referred to a registered community housing provider by the agency; or
immediately before becoming a tenant of a registered community housing provider—
was a tenant or prospective tenant of Kāinga Ora housing; and
had not been assessed by the agency as being no longer eligible for social housing; and
an income-related rent has been calculated for the tenant under Part 9 and the calculation mechanism; and
the agency has notified under section 106 the income-related rent calculated for the tenant; and

that notification of the income-related rent has not been replaced by a notification from the agency to the effect that the rent for the housing may be determined by the registered community housing provider but must not exceed its market rent for the time being.

If subsection (1) applies to any community housing, a registered community housing provider, and a tenant, the rent for the community housing must be the income-related rent for the time being notified by the agency, subject to subsections (4) and (5).

If subsection (1) does not apply to any community housing, a registered community housing provider, and a tenant, the rent for the housing may be determined by the registered community housing provider, but must not exceed its market rent for the time being.
The income-related rent for any community housing must not exceed its market rent for the time being.

If a registered community housing provider is satisfied that special circumstances justify its doing so, it may, in its absolute discretion, set for and accept from a tenant a rent lower than the rent otherwise required by subsection (2) to be paid for the housing by the tenant.
Subsections (2) to (5) are subject to section 93.

In subsection (1), eligible tenant means a tenant who is eligible to be allocated, assigned, or let community housing at an income-related rent on the basis of ministerial directions issued under section 102.

history.history-note: Section 92: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).
Section 92(1)﻿(a)﻿(ii)﻿(A): amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).

prov.heading: Changes in rent

para.text: This section applies to an existing tenant of community housing if the registered community housing provider—
is required by section 92 or 94 to change the rent for the housing; or
is empowered by section 92 to change the rent for the housing, and decides to do so.

The registered community housing provider must, in accordance with section 136 of the Residential Tenancies Act 1986, give a tenant to whom subsection (1) applies written notice of the change in rent, stating—
the day on which it takes effect; and
the matters referred to in paragraphs (j) and (k) of section 13A of that Act.
In the case of a reduction in rent, the notice has effect as a variation of the tenancy agreement for the housing whether or not the tenant signs it.
In the case of an increase in rent, the notice must comply with section 24 of the Residential Tenancies Act 1986, except—

subsections (1)﻿(d) to (h) and (1A) of that section do not apply to the increase in rent if the registered community housing provider has received a notification from the agency under sections 106 and 118A(3) of this Act that the rent for the housing may be determined by the provider but must not exceed its market rent for the time being:
to the extent that section 94 of this Act provides otherwise, in any other case.

history.history-note: Section 93: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).
Section 93(4): replaced, on 2 March 2026, by section 21 of the Social Assistance Legislation (Accommodation Supplement and Income-related Rent) Amendment Act 2025 (2025 No 27).

prov.heading: Increase in rent due to change in tenant's circumstances

para.text: If a registered community housing provider receives a notification from the agency under section 106 of an increase in an income-related rent due to a change in the tenant’s circumstances or the circumstances of an applicable person in relation to the tenant,—

the provider must, subject to subsection (4), give the tenant written notice, in accordance with section 93(2), stating the date on which the increased income-related rent takes effect (the effective date); and
the effective date must be a date no earlier than 61 days after the date (as stated in the agency's notification) that the change in circumstances occurred; and
the provider must give the notice to the tenant at least 2 weeks before the effective date.
The agency may recover from the tenant as a debt due to the Crown, the difference (if any) between—

the higher income-related rent calculated by the agency for the tenant for the housing in respect of the period commencing on the date that is 61 days after the change in circumstances occurred and ending on the day before the effective date (capped at the market rent); and
the income-related rent applying to that period that the agency had calculated for the tenant for the housing and set out in its previous notification.
The income-related rent notified under subsection (1)﻿(a) for the housing must not exceed its market rent for the time being.

A registered community housing provider must change the rent charged following receipt of a notification from the agency unless, in the provider’s opinion, it would not result in a material difference to the rent charged for the housing concerned.
Section 24(1)﻿(c) to (h) and 24(1A) of the Residential Tenancies Act 1986 do not apply to the increase in rent.
Amounts recoverable under subsection (2) are not rent in arrear for the purposes of the Residential Tenancies Act 1986.

Mary is a tenant of a registered community housing provider and is paying an income-related rent to the provider. On 1 March, Mary returned to full-time employment but did not tell the agency of her change in circumstances.

Several months later, the agency becomes aware that Mary’s circumstances had changed on 1 March. The agency calculates an increased income-related rent for Mary, and notifies the registered community housing provider accordingly. The increased income-related rent does not exceed the market rent for the housing.

On 1 October that same year, the registered community housing provider notifies Mary that she must pay an increased income-related rent (in accordance with the agency’s notification) from the rent period beginning on 21 October (the effective date).
The agency is entitled to recover from Mary, as a debt due to the Crown, the difference between—

the higher income-related rent for the housing (as set out in the agency’s notification) in respect of the period commencing on 1 May (61 days after the change in circumstances) and ending on 20 October; and
the income-related rent applying to that period that the agency had calculated for the tenant for the housing (as set out in the agency's previous notification).

history.history-note: Section 94: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).
Section 94(5): amended, on 11 February 2021, by section 82 of the Residential Tenancies Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 59).

prov.heading: Placement in community housing

para.text: The matters to which a registered community housing provider may have regard in doing any of the things stated in subsection (2) may include criteria that have, or are capable of having, the effect that tenants, people who are or might be applicable persons in relation to those tenants, and other people who are or might be residing in the housing concerned are treated differently on the basis of—

their marital status, disability or absence of disability, age, or family status (as the terms marital status, disability, age, and family status are defined in paragraphs (b), (h), (i), and (l) of section 21(1) of the Human Rights Act 1993); or
whether or not they are resident, or ordinarily resident, or permanently resident, or lawfully resident, in New Zealand; or
their incomes; or
their property; or
2 or more of those factors.

The things are anything that a registered community housing provider does in the course of allocating, assigning, and letting community housing to tenants, and in administering and terminating those tenancies, and include—
allocating, assigning, and letting community housing to tenants at an income-related rent; and
allocating, assigning, and letting community housing to tenants; and
reviewing the eligibility of a tenant to be or to continue to be allocated, assigned, or let particular community housing; and
terminating a tenancy; and
reallocating or reassigning community housing to tenants; and
retaining the current allocation, assignment, or letting of particular community housing to tenants.
Nothing in this section affects the application of the New Zealand Bill of Rights Act 1990.

history.history-note: Section 95: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: Registered community housing provider may review placement

para.text: A registered community housing provider may—
allocate a prospective tenant for community housing to particular community housing provided by that provider:
review the eligibility of a tenant to be or to continue to be allocated, assigned, or let particular community housing provided by that provider:

require a tenant to transfer to different community housing provided by that provider if the provider considers that the transfer is necessary or desirable for any reason and the other housing is appropriate to the tenant's housing needs.
Nothing in this section limits or affects—
a tenant’s rights under the Residential Tenancies Act 1986:
a registered community housing provider’s rights as a landlord under the Residential Tenancies Act 1986, including its rights to terminate a tenancy in accordance with that Act.

history.history-note: Section 96: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: Limits on obligations of registered community housing provider

para.text: Nothing in this Act requires a registered community housing provider to provide any housing or particular housing to a tenant referred or allocated to the provider by the agency.

history.history-note: Section 97: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: Reimbursement agreements with registered community housing providers

para.text: The Crown may require a registered community housing provider to enter into 1 or more agreements for the provision, by that provider, of social housing and related services to any persons paying income-related rents in return for the payment by the Crown of the price to the provider of doing so (being the difference between the amount of market rents for the housing and the income-related rents notified to the provider by the agency).

However, subsection (1) does not apply to the extent (if any) that a tailored agreement covers the provision by the registered community housing provider of social housing and related services to any persons paying income-related rents. (See section 137A.)

history.history-note: Section 98: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).
Section 98 heading: amended, on 28 May 2015, by section 7(1) of the Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters (Social Housing Reform) Amendment Act 2015 (2015 No 50).
Section 98(2): inserted, on 28 May 2015, by section 7(2) of the Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters (Social Housing Reform) Amendment Act 2015 (2015 No 50).

part.heading: Social housing agency

history.history-note: Part 9: inserted, on 28 November 2013, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).
Heading: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: Interpretation of terms used in this Part

para.text: In this Part, unless the context otherwise requires,—
appeal body means the body specified in regulations made under section 136(1) to whom appeals under section 133 may be made
Ministry means the department for the time being responsible for the administration of the Social Security Act 2018
prescribed means for the time being—
prescribed by the calculation mechanism for the purposes of the provision; or
calculated under a means prescribed by the calculation mechanism for the purposes of the provision.

history.history-note: Section 99: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).
Section 99 Ministry: amended, on 26 November 2018, by section 459 of the Social Security Act 2018 (2018 No 32).

part.crosshead: Appointment, functions, and operation of social housing agency

history.history-note: Heading: inserted, on 28 November 2013, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: Social housing agency

para.text: The Governor-General may, from time to time, by Order in Council made on the recommendation of the joint Ministers, appoint a department, departmental agency, or a Crown entity or any combination of 1 or more departments, departmental agencies, or Crown entities, to perform some or all of the functions and exercise some or all of the powers of the agency under this Act.
An order under this section is secondary legislation (see Part 3 of the Legislation Act 2019 for publication requirements).

legtable.summary: The following table is small in size and has 3 columns that are grouped under the heading Legislation Act 2019 requirements for secondary legislation made under this section.

entry.emphasis: Legislation Act 2019 requirements for secondary legislation made under this section

row.entry: PCO must publish it on the legislation website and notify it in the Gazette
The Minister must present it to the House of Representatives
It may be disallowed by the House of Representatives

entry.emphasis: This note is not part of the Act.

history.history-note: Section 100: inserted, on 28 November 2013, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).
Section 100(2): inserted, on 28 October 2021, by section 3 of the Secondary Legislation Act 2021 (2021 No 7).

prov.heading: Functions of agency

para.text: The functions of the agency include the following:
providing assistance and advice to people on matters relating to housing or services related to housing; and
managing applications for social housing, including—
assessing the eligibility of prospective tenants to be allocated social housing or to be referred or allocated to any particular social housing provider:
assessing the housing needs of prospective tenants:
reviewing the eligibility of tenants to continue to be allocated social housing and reviewing their housing needs:
operating a waiting list or waiting lists of prospective tenants who have been assessed as being eligible for social housing, but who have yet to be allocated, assigned, or let social housing:
referring or allocating prospective tenants to social housing providers:
providing the results of its assessments of eligibility and housing needs to Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities or registered community housing providers; and
any other functions of the agency set out in this Act and any other functions under Part 7 that are delegated to the agency.

history.history-note: Section 101: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).
Section 101(b)﻿(vi): amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).

prov.heading: Ministerial directions to agency

para.text: The joint Ministers and, if applicable, any other Minister responsible for the agency may give to the agency directions setting out the terms and conditions on which the funding by way of payments under reimbursement agreements and tailored agreements must be made available to registered community housing providers and Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities, including—
the type of housing units that may be funded during any specified period of time; or
the number of housing units that may be funded during any specified period of time; or
the location of housing units that may be funded during any specified period of time.

The Minister responsible for the agency may, in consultation with the joint Ministers and for the following purposes, give to the agency directions in accordance with which the agency may enter into a tailored agreement under section 137A.

The purposes of a direction under subsection (1A) are to enable flexible purchasing approaches by the agency and to promote social housing outcomes that reflect the diverse needs of tenants and the diverse range of existing social housing providers and potential providers.
Without limiting the generality of subsection (1A), a direction under that subsection may—
relate to any 1 or more social housing providers or to any particular social housing provider or providers:
relate to all social housing and related services, or state the social housing and related services to which it relates (for example, by type or number or location):

relate to the provision of social housing now and into the future whether or not the social housing services sought relate to existing or anticipated premises or to premises currently owned by the social housing provider.
The joint Ministers, and if applicable, any other Minister responsible for the agency must give to the agency directions stating the criteria by which the agency must assess—
a person's eligibility for social housing:
a person's continued eligibility for social housing.
The joint Ministers, and if applicable, any other Minister responsible for the agency may give to the agency directions determining—
the timing of reviews by the agency under section 117; and
the class or classes of people which the agency may or may not review under that section.
Before making any direction under subsection (1), (2), or (3), the joint Ministers and, if applicable, any other Minister responsible for the agency must consult—
the agency and the authority; and
any social housing provider that, in the Minister's opinion, will be materially affected by the direction.
The requirement in subsection (4)﻿(b) does not apply to the first directions to be made under subsection (2) or (3).

The agency must, in carrying out functions under this Act, give effect to any directions that are given to it under this section and that are signed by the Minister or Ministers who may give the directions.
If the agency is a Crown entity, subsections (1) to (6) do not limit Part 3 of the Crown Entities Act 2004.
If there is any inconsistency between the provisions of this Act and the Crown Entities Act 2004 relating to ministerial directions, the provisions of the Crown Entities Act 2004 prevail.
A direction under this section is secondary legislation (see Part 3 of the Legislation Act 2019 for publication requirements).

legtable.summary: The following table is small in size and has 3 columns that are grouped under the heading Legislation Act 2019 requirements for secondary legislation made under this section.

entry.emphasis: Legislation Act 2019 requirements for secondary legislation made under this section

row.entry: The maker must publish it in the Gazette
The Minister must present it to the House of Representatives
It may be disallowed by the House of Representatives

entry.emphasis: This note is not part of the Act.

history.history-note: Section 102: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).
Section 102(1): amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Section 102(1): amended, on 28 May 2015, by section 8(1)﻿(a) of the Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters (Social Housing Reform) Amendment Act 2015 (2015 No 50).
Section 102(1)﻿(a): amended, on 28 May 2015, by section 8(1)﻿(b) of the Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters (Social Housing Reform) Amendment Act 2015 (2015 No 50).
Section 102(1)﻿(b): amended, on 28 May 2015, by section 8(1)﻿(b) of the Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters (Social Housing Reform) Amendment Act 2015 (2015 No 50).
Section 102(1)﻿(c): amended, on 28 May 2015, by section 8(1)﻿(b) of the Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters (Social Housing Reform) Amendment Act 2015 (2015 No 50).
Section 102(1A): inserted, on 28 May 2015, by section 8(2) of the Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters (Social Housing Reform) Amendment Act 2015 (2015 No 50).
Section 102(1B): inserted, on 28 May 2015, by section 8(2) of the Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters (Social Housing Reform) Amendment Act 2015 (2015 No 50).
Section 102(1C): inserted, on 28 May 2015, by section 8(2) of the Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters (Social Housing Reform) Amendment Act 2015 (2015 No 50).
Section 102(4): amended, on 28 May 2015, by section 8(3) of the Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters (Social Housing Reform) Amendment Act 2015 (2015 No 50).
Section 102(6): replaced, on 28 May 2015, by section 8(4) of the Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters (Social Housing Reform) Amendment Act 2015 (2015 No 50).
Section 102(9): replaced, on 28 October 2021, by section 3 of the Secondary Legislation Act 2021 (2021 No 7).

part.crosshead: Eligibility, housing needs, and income-related rent

history.history-note: Heading: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: Agency to notify social housing providers of eligibility and housing needs

para.text: The agency must, in relation to any tenant of a social housing provider that has been referred or allocated to the provider by the agency or is housed by the provider, notify the provider each time the agency makes an assessment of—
the eligibility or continued eligibility of the tenant for social housing; and
the housing needs of the tenant.
The notification—
must set out the results of the agency's assessment; and
may include any other information that the agency considers reasonable or necessary, including matters relevant to tenancy sustainability; and

must include the information reasonably requested by the social housing provider, to enable the social housing provider to make decisions on allocating, assigning, and letting housing, and continuing to allocate, assign, and let housing.

The agency must update the notification following any determination that affects the tenant’s eligibility for social housing or housing needs assessed by the agency or determined on review and appeal, and may update a notification following an investigation under this Part.
A social housing provider is entitled to rely on a notification given under this section.
A notification under this section may be given by electronic means in accordance with subpart 2 of Part 4 of the Contract and Commercial Law Act 2017.

history.history-note: Section 103: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).
Section 103(5): amended, on 1 September 2017, by section 347 of the Contract and Commercial Law Act 2017 (2017 No 5).

prov.heading: Income-related rent

para.text: This subsection applies to social housing and a tenant if—
the tenant is eligible to be allocated social housing or has been allocated, assigned, or let particular social housing; and
the tenant has applied to the agency for it to calculate an income-related rent; and
the agency is satisfied that—
it has had all information reasonably needed to calculate or review an income-related rent for long enough to be able to do so; and
the information is accurate.
If subsection (1) applies, the agency must—
calculate the income-related rent for the time being for the tenant; and
provide notification under section 106.

However, if the agency is not satisfied that it has had all information reasonably needed to calculate or review an income-related rent for long enough to be able to do so or that the information is accurate, the agency must provide notification to the social housing provider under section 106 that, as applicable,—
the rent for the housing must be its market rent for the time being, in accordance with section 72(3); or
the rent for the housing may be determined by the registered community housing provider, but must not exceed its market rent for the time being, in accordance with section 92(3).
The agency must update a notification following any change to the income-related rent calculated or determined for the tenant.
The agency may update a notification following an investigation of circumstances relevant to an income-related rent under this Part.

history.history-note: Section 104: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: Backdating of applications for calculation of income-related rent

para.text: The agency may treat an application made at any time for it to calculate an income-related rent as having been made at any earlier time it determines, if satisfied that—
it has all information reasonably needed to calculate an income-related rent as at the time determined; and
the information is accurate; and
it was unreasonable in all the circumstances to expect the application to have been made earlier.
This subsection applies to an application made to the agency for it to calculate an income-related rent if,—
at the time it was made, the agency—
did not have all the information reasonably needed to calculate an income-related rent; or
had all the information reasonably needed to calculate an income-related rent, but was not satisfied that it was accurate; and
at some later time, the agency is satisfied that—
it has all that information; and
the information is accurate.

The agency may treat an application to which subsection (2) applies as if the agency had had all the information reasonably needed to calculate an income-related rent at any time it determines between the time the application was made and the later time concerned.

history.history-note: Section 105: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: Agency must notify social housing providers of tenant’s income-related rent

para.text: The agency must notify a social housing provider of the income-related rent calculated for a tenant who has been referred or allocated to, or is housed by, that social housing provider.
The agency—

must update the notification following any change to the income-related rent calculated or notified for the tenant, including any change where the agency's assessment is that the income-related rent should be equal to the market rent; and
may update a notification following an investigation under this Part.
An updated notification must state—

whether the change in income-related rent is due to a change in the tenant’s circumstances or the circumstances of any other applicable person and, if so, the date the change in circumstances occurred; and
whether the change in income-related rent is required or permitted under section 118A(3).
A social housing provider is entitled to rely on a notification given under this section.
A notification under this section may be given by electronic means in accordance with subpart 2 of Part 4 of the Contract and Commercial Law Act 2017.

history.history-note: Section 106: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).
Section 106(3): replaced, on 2 March 2026, by section 22 of the Social Assistance Legislation (Accommodation Supplement and Income-related Rent) Amendment Act 2025 (2025 No 27).
Section 106(5): amended, on 1 September 2017, by section 347 of the Contract and Commercial Law Act 2017 (2017 No 5).
Heading: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: Calculating income-related rents

para.text: An income-related rent (calculated on a weekly basis) is the higher of the following rents:
a rent calculated under subsection (2):
a rent calculated under subsection (3).
A rent referred to in subsection (1)﻿(a) is the sum of—

the prescribed proportion of the sum of the assessable incomes of the applicable persons concerned, up to the prescribed threshold (or the threshold prescribed for tenants of a category to which the tenant belongs); and
the prescribed proportion of any amount by which that sum is greater than that threshold; and
if any of the applicable persons is eligible to receive family tax credits under subparts MA to MF and MZ of the Income Tax Act 2007, the prescribed proportion of the lesser of—
the total of the amounts that the applicable persons are eligible to receive as family tax credits under those subparts; and
a prescribed amount (or an amount calculated by a prescribed means); and
if the applicable persons receive contributions from additional residents, 62% of the sum of the contributions from the additional residents.
A rent referred to in subsection (1)﻿(b) is the sum of—

the prescribed proportion of the rate (before reduction on account of income) of jobseeker support stated in clause 1(b) to (j) of Part 1 of Schedule 4 of the Social Security Act 2018 that would be appropriate if the tenant were a beneficiary; and
if any of the applicable persons is eligible to receive family tax credits under subparts MA to MF and MZ of the Income Tax Act 2007, the prescribed proportion of the lesser of—
the total of the amounts that the applicable persons are eligible to receive as family tax credits under those subparts; and
a prescribed amount (or an amount calculated by a prescribed means); and
if the applicable persons receive contributions from additional residents, 62% of the sum of the contributions from the additional residents.

history.history-note: Section 107: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).
Section 107(1)﻿(a): amended, on 2 March 2026, by section 23(1) of the Social Assistance Legislation (Accommodation Supplement and Income-related Rent) Amendment Act 2025 (2025 No 27).
Section 107(1)﻿(b): amended, on 2 March 2026, by section 23(2) of the Social Assistance Legislation (Accommodation Supplement and Income-related Rent) Amendment Act 2025 (2025 No 27).
Section 107(2): amended, on 2 March 2026, by section 23(3) of the Social Assistance Legislation (Accommodation Supplement and Income-related Rent) Amendment Act 2025 (2025 No 27).
Section 107(2)﻿(d): inserted, on 2 March 2026, by section 23(4) of the Social Assistance Legislation (Accommodation Supplement and Income-related Rent) Amendment Act 2025 (2025 No 27).
Section 107(3): amended, on 2 March 2026, by section 23(5) of the Social Assistance Legislation (Accommodation Supplement and Income-related Rent) Amendment Act 2025 (2025 No 27).
Section 107(3)﻿(a): amended, on 26 November 2018, by section 459 of the Social Security Act 2018 (2018 No 32).
