Section 107(3)﻿(c): inserted, on 2 March 2026, by section 23(6) of the Social Assistance Legislation (Accommodation Supplement and Income-related Rent) Amendment Act 2025 (2025 No 27).

prov.heading: Assessable income

para.text: The assessable income of an applicable person is the agency's estimate of the person's weekly income from all sources,—
if the agency considers that income tax is payable on any part of it, after the deduction of whichever of the following the agency thinks fit in the particular case:
any income tax actually paid in respect of or deducted from that part; or
the agency's estimate of the amount of income tax payable in respect of it; and

if the agency considers that any premium is payable in respect of any part of it under section 219(1) of the Accident Compensation Act 2001, after the deduction of whichever of the following the agency thinks fit in the particular case:
any premium actually paid in respect of that part; or
the agency's estimate of the amount of premium payable in respect of it; and

if the agency considers that any levy is payable in respect of any part of it under section 219 of the Accident Compensation Act 2001, after the deduction of whichever of the following the agency thinks fit in the particular case:
any levy actually paid in respect of that part; or
the agency's estimate of the amount of levy payable in respect of it.

history.history-note: Section 108: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: Certain amounts included in weekly income

para.text: For the purposes of section 108, a person's weekly income includes—

the appropriate weekly proportion of any periodical payment, whether capital or not, made to the person on a regular basis by any other person for income-related purposes and used by the person for income-related purposes; and

the appropriate weekly proportion of the value of any goods, service, transport, or accommodation (other than accommodation provided by Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities or a registered community housing provider) supplied to the person on a regular basis by any other person.
Subsection (1) does not limit section 108.

history.history-note: Section 109: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).
Section 109(1)﻿(b): amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Section 109(2): repealed, on 2 March 2026, by section 24 of the Social Assistance Legislation (Accommodation Supplement and Income-related Rent) Amendment Act 2025 (2025 No 27).
Section 109(4): repealed, on 2 March 2026, by section 24 of the Social Assistance Legislation (Accommodation Supplement and Income-related Rent) Amendment Act 2025 (2025 No 27).

prov.heading: Calculation mechanism may include amounts in or exclude amounts from weekly income

para.text: For the purposes of section 108, a person's weekly income—
includes any amount or payment (or, as the case requires, the appropriate weekly proportion of any amount or payment) of a prescribed description:
does not include any amount or payment (or, as the case requires, the appropriate weekly proportion of any amount or payment) of a prescribed description.
Subsection (1)﻿(a) does not limit section 108.
Subsection (1)﻿(b) overrides sections 108 and 109(1).

history.history-note: Section 110: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

part.crosshead: Assessable assets, and deprivation of income or property

history.history-note: Heading: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: Assessable assets

para.text: Assessable assets generate imputed income at a rate of interest for the time being stated by the joint Ministers by notice; and, for the purposes of section 108,—
the gross income from any person's assessable assets is the greater of the actual income from those assets and the imputed income they generate; and
the person's weekly income must be estimated accordingly.
In subsection (1), assessable assets has the meaning prescribed in regulations made under section 114.
A notice under this section is secondary legislation (see Part 3 of the Legislation Act 2019 for publication requirements).

legtable.summary: The following table is small in size and has 3 columns that are grouped under the heading Legislation Act 2019 requirements for secondary legislation made under this section.

entry.emphasis: Legislation Act 2019 requirements for secondary legislation made under this section

row.entry: The maker must publish it in the Gazette
It is not required to be presented to the House of Representatives because a transitional exemption applies under Schedule 1 of the Legislation Act 2019
It may be disallowed by the House of Representatives

entry.emphasis: This note is not part of the Act.

history.history-note: Section 111: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).
Section 111(1): amended, on 28 October 2021, by section 3 of the Secondary Legislation Act 2021 (2021 No 7).
Section 111(3): inserted, on 28 October 2021, by section 3 of the Secondary Legislation Act 2021 (2021 No 7).

prov.heading: Assessable income may be adjusted in certain cases

para.text: Subsection (2) applies if the agency is satisfied that—
an applicable person has directly or indirectly deprived himself or herself of any income or property; and

as a consequence of the deprivation, an income-related rent of any social housing in respect of which the person is an applicable person is (or but for this section would be) lower than it would otherwise be.
For the purposes of this section, deprivation of income, property, or both, by an applicable person excludes that applicable person doing all or any of—
not applying for a formula assessment of child support payable in respect of a qualifying child under the Child Support Act 1991:
electing to end a formula assessment of child support payable in respect of a qualifying child under that Act:
choosing not to enter into a voluntary agreement for child support in respect of a qualifying child under that Act (whether or not that voluntary agreement—
is a voluntary agreement that, under section 48 of that Act, qualifies for acceptance by the Commissioner of Inland Revenue; and so
is a qualifying voluntary agreement (as defined in section 2(1) of that Act)):
making under that Act an election to terminate liability under a qualifying voluntary agreement that, under section 48 of that Act, has been accepted by the Commissioner of Inland Revenue:

not making, or making, any other arrangement (for example, one in respect of a qualifying child and that is not a qualifying voluntary agreement (as those terms are defined in section 2(1) of that Act)) that does or may affect whether or what child support is payable in respect of a qualifying child under that Act.
Subsection (1A) does not apply to, and does not limit the application of section 9 (UCB beneficiaries must apply for formula assessment) of the Child Support Act 1991 to,—
an applicant for an unsupported child’s benefit; or
the spouse or partner of that applicant; or
a UCB beneficiary (as defined in section 2(1) of the Child Support Act 1991); or
the spouse or partner of that UCB beneficiary.
The agency may treat the person's assessable income as having been increased to the extent the agency thinks necessary to reflect the deprivation, or any lesser extent.
Subsection (2) overrides sections 107(1) and 113.
In this section,—
income does not include any amount that an applicable person might be considered to derive from any impairment lump sum under Schedule 1 of the Accident Compensation Act 2001
property does not include any such impairment lump sum received by an applicable person for the period of 12 months following the receipt of that payment.

history.history-note: Section 112: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).
Section 112(1A): inserted, on 1 July 2023, by section 64 of the Child Support (Pass On) Acts Amendment Act 2023 (2023 No 27).
Section 112(1B): inserted, on 1 July 2023, by section 64 of the Child Support (Pass On) Acts Amendment Act 2023 (2023 No 27).

part.crosshead: Estimating weekly income, and regulations for purposes of calculation mechanism

history.history-note: Heading: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: Estimating weekly income

para.text: For the purposes of section 108, a person's weekly income is the person's estimated income for the prescribed period commencing on the day on which the estimation is made (or in the case of an application backdated under section 105 or a review under section 116 or action taken under section 123, the day from which the estimation is made), divided so as to equate to a weekly amount; but—
that estimated income must be treated as an amount equal to—

the agency's estimate of the income received by the person for any period equal to the prescribed period (ending on a day before the day concerned) that the agency decides, divided so as to equate to a weekly amount; or

if the agency thinks it more appropriate in all the circumstances, the agency's estimate of the income received by the person for any shorter period (ending on a day before the day concerned) that the agency decides, divided so as to equate to a weekly amount; and

there may be deducted from that amount any items by which the agency is satisfied the income is likely to be reduced, and there may be added to that amount any items by which the agency is satisfied the income is likely to be increased.

history.history-note: Section 113: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: Regulations for purposes of calculation mechanism

para.text: The Governor-General may, by Order in Council, make regulations for any or all of the following purposes:
prescribing a definition of assessable assets for the purposes of section 111(2):
prescribing matters contemplated by sections 107 to 113:
providing for any other matters contemplated by sections 107 to 113, necessary for their administration, or necessary for giving them full effect.
The regulations may—
prescribe a zero proportion (or a means for calculating proportions capable of producing a zero proportion) for the purposes of any provision:
prescribe a proportion or threshold (or a means for calculating a proportion or threshold) for the purposes of any provision by reference to any or all of the following matters:
the relationship status of the tenant concerned:
the relationship status of the applicable persons concerned:
the number of dependent children of the tenant concerned:
the number of dependent children of the applicable persons concerned:
the number of people living or intended to live in the housing concerned:

prescribe a threshold (or a means for calculating a threshold) for the purposes of any provision by reference to the rate of a benefit as defined in Schedule 2 of the Social Security Act 2018, or the rates of 2 or more such benefits.
A means for calculating may comprise any number of mechanisms and parameters.
Subsection (2) does not limit subsection (1).
Nothing in this section affects the application of the New Zealand Bill of Rights Act 1990.
Regulations under this section are secondary legislation (see Part 3 of the Legislation Act 2019 for publication requirements).

legtable.summary: The following table is small in size and has 3 columns that are grouped under the heading Legislation Act 2019 requirements for secondary legislation made under this section.

entry.emphasis: Legislation Act 2019 requirements for secondary legislation made under this section

row.entry: PCO must publish it on the legislation website and notify it in the Gazette
The Minister must present it to the House of Representatives
It may be disallowed by the House of Representatives

entry.emphasis: This note is not part of the Act.

history.history-note: Section 114: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).
Section 114(2)﻿(c): amended, on 26 November 2018, by section 459 of the Social Security Act 2018 (2018 No 32).
Section 114(6): inserted, on 28 October 2021, by section 3 of the Secondary Legislation Act 2021 (2021 No 7).
Heading: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: Tenant's duty to advise changes of circumstances

para.text: Every person to whom any social housing is let at an income-related rent must promptly advise the agency of—
any change in the person's circumstances likely to result in the payment of a higher income-related rent; and
any change known to the person in the circumstances of any other applicable person likely to result in the payment of a higher income-related rent by the first-mentioned person.
Every person to whom any social housing is let (whether at an income-related rent or a market rent) must promptly advise the agency of—

any change in the person's circumstances likely to result in the person no longer being eligible to be allocated social housing or no longer in need of the particular social housing the person is being let; and
any change known to the person in the circumstances of any other applicable person likely to result in the first-mentioned person no longer—
being eligible to be allocated social housing; or
being in need of the particular social housing that the first-mentioned person is being let.

Every person who is a prospective tenant must promptly advise the agency of any change in the circumstances of the person or the person's spouse or partner likely to result in the person not or no longer—
being eligible to be allocated social housing; or
needing to be allocated, assigned, or let any particular social housing.

history.history-note: Section 115: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).
Section 115(2): brought into force, on 14 April 2016, by section 2(1) of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.label: 115A
prov.heading: How duty to advise satisfied for information share child support payment made to person

prov.body.subprov.crosshead: How section 115(1), (2), or (3) is satisfied for payment

para.text: Section 115(1), (2), or (3) is satisfied for a change in a person’s circumstances that is an information share child support payment made to the person if the payment is disclosed to the agency under an approved information sharing agreement (as defined in section 138 of the Privacy Act 2020) between—
the Inland Revenue Department (as the department responsible for the administration of the Child Support Act 1991); and
the agency (as the department, departmental agency, or Crown entity responsible for the administration of provisions of this Act).

prov.body.subprov.crosshead: Exception if person does not promptly advise of matter relevant to review

para.text: However, subsection (1) does not apply if—
the agency has in some way notified the person that the payment has been disclosed to the agency under an approved information sharing agreement (see the examples given in subsection (3)); and
the person does not promptly advise the agency of a change in the person’s circumstances that relates to the payment, and that is or may be relevant to a review under all or any of—
section 116 (reviews of income-related rents):
section 117 (agency may review housing eligibility):
section 118 (agency may review housing needs of tenant).

example.heading: Examples of change in circumstances that relates to payment

para.text: Examples of a change in the person’s circumstances that relates to the payment include that—
the person did not receive all or any of the payment:
the payment as received by the person is of a smaller amount than the amount of the payment as disclosed to the agency under an approved information sharing agreement:
the person has been incorrectly identified as the proper recipient of the payment.

prov.body.subprov.crosshead: Examples of agency notifying person that payment disclosed to agency

para.text: For the purposes of subsection (2)﻿(a), examples of the agency in some way notifying the person that the payment has been disclosed to the agency under an approved information sharing agreement include the agency notifying the person—
that information share child support payments are, or will be, disclosed to the agency under an approved information sharing agreement; or
that 1 or more particular information share child support payments disclosed to the agency under an approved information sharing agreement are or may be relevant to—
a review proposed to be conducted under all or any of sections 116 to 118; or
any outcome (for example, any proposed outcome) of a review conducted under all or any of sections 116 to 118.

prov.body.subprov.crosshead: Meaning of information share child support payment

para.text: In this section, information share child support payment—
has the same meaning as in Schedule 2 of the Social Security Act 2018; and therefore
excludes a general provisions child support payment (as that term is defined in Schedule 2 of the Social Security Act 2018).

history.history-note: Section 115A: inserted, on 1 July 2023, by section 65 of the Child Support (Pass On) Acts Amendment Act 2023 (2023 No 27).

prov.heading: Reviews of income-related rents

para.text: The agency may at any time, of its own motion or on application by a tenant of the social housing concerned, review any income-related rent to ascertain—
whether it or some other income-related rent is now appropriate; or
whether at some earlier time it or some other income-related rent was appropriate; or
both.
After reviewing the income-related rent, the agency,—

if satisfied some other income-related rent (including an income-related rent equivalent to the market rent) is now appropriate, may calculate and notify that other income-related rent for the tenant:
if satisfied that at some earlier time a higher income-related rent was appropriate, may take action under section 74(3), 94(2), or 127:
if satisfied that at some earlier time a lower income-related rent was appropriate, must make any necessary refund.

However, if after reviewing the income-related rent, the agency is not satisfied that it has all information reasonably needed to calculate or review an income-related rent, the agency must provide notification to the social housing provider under section 106 that, as applicable,—
the rent for the housing must be its market rent for the time being, in accordance with section 72(3); or
the rent for the housing may be determined by the registered community housing provider, but must not exceed its market rent for the time being, in accordance with section 92(3).
The agency—
does not have to calculate that other income-related rent under subsection (2)﻿(a) unless, in the agency's opinion, it would result in a material difference to the existing income-related rent; and

does not have to give a notification under section 103 or 106 unless the agency has calculated the income-related rent and, in the agency's opinion, the result is a material difference from the existing income-related rent; and
does not have to act under subsection (2)﻿(b) unless in the agency's opinion, there has been a material under-payment of rent for the housing concerned.

history.history-note: Section 116: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: Agency may review housing eligibility

para.text: The agency may—
at any time, on its own motion or on the application of a social housing provider, review the eligibility of a prospective tenant for social housing:

subject to and in accordance with any directions given under section 102(3), at any time, on its own motion or on the application of a social housing provider, review the continued eligibility of a tenant for social housing.
The agency must conduct a review under subsection (1)﻿(a) in accordance with directions given under section 102(2).
The agency must conduct a review under subsection (1)﻿(b) in accordance with directions (if any) given under section 102(2)﻿(b).
Nothing in this section limits or affects any power of the agency to conduct a review under any other enactment.

history.history-note: Section 117: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: Agency may review housing needs of tenant

para.text: The agency may, at any time, on its own motion or on the application of a social housing provider, review the housing needs of a tenant.
Nothing in this section limits or affects any power of the agency to conduct a review under any other enactment.

history.history-note: Section 118: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.label: 118A
prov.heading: Discrepancies in information received relevant to income-related rent

para.text: This section applies if—
the agency identifies a discrepancy between—
information received by the agency from a tenant of social housing (A) who pays an income-related rent; and
information received by the agency from another person (B) who is receiving housing assistance; and
the discrepancy is in information that is relevant for the purposes of ascertaining either or both of the following:
whether the income-related rent that A is paying or some other income-related rent is now appropriate:
whether the income-related rent that A was paying at some earlier time or some other income-related rent was appropriate; and
the discrepancy is not resolved to the agency’s satisfaction, within 1 working day after the agency identified the discrepancy, as a result of the agency doing either or both of the following:
checking with A that the information that the agency received from A correctly reflects what A intended to provide to the agency:
checking with B that the information that the agency received from B correctly reflects what B intended to provide to the agency.
The agency must notify A and B of the particulars of the discrepancy as soon as reasonably practicable.

If the discrepancy is not resolved to the agency’s satisfaction within 10 working days from the date on which the agency notified A and B of the particulars of the discrepancy, the agency must, on the first working day after the end of that 10-working-day period, provide notification to the social housing provider under section 106 that, as applicable,—
the rent for the housing must be its market rent for the time being, in accordance with section 72(3); or
the rent for the housing may be determined by the registered community housing provider but must not exceed its market rent for the time being, in accordance with section 92(3).
If there is an inconsistency between this section and another provision of this Act, this section prevails.
In this section, housing assistance has the same meaning as in section 108A(6) of the Social Security Act 2018.

history.history-note: Section 118A: inserted, on 2 March 2026, by section 25 of the Social Assistance Legislation (Accommodation Supplement and Income-related Rent) Amendment Act 2025 (2025 No 27).
Heading: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: Agency may investigate circumstances relevant to income-related rent

para.text: The agency may investigate—
the present circumstances of—
any tenant of social housing who is paying, or has applied to the agency for the agency to calculate, an income-related rent; or
any person who is an applicable person in relation to the tenant:
the circumstances (as they existed immediately before the income-related rent concerned was calculated or during any period when it was applicable) of—
any tenant or former tenant of social housing who was required to pay an income-related rent for the housing; or
any person who was an applicable person in relation to the tenant or former tenant at the time concerned.

history.history-note: Section 119: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: Agency may investigate circumstances of prospective tenant

para.text: The agency may investigate the circumstances of any prospective tenant or of any person who would be an applicable person in relation to that tenant to the extent that those circumstances might be relevant to—
the eligibility of the prospective tenant to be allocated social housing or to be allocated or referred to any particular social housing provider; or
the housing needs of the prospective tenant.

history.history-note: Section 120: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: Agency may investigate circumstances relevant to continued eligibility

para.text: The agency may investigate the circumstances of any tenant of social housing (whether paying income-related rent or market rent for the housing) or the circumstances of any applicable person in relation to that tenant, to the extent that those circumstances might be relevant to—
the continued eligibility of the tenant to be allocated social housing; or
the housing needs of the tenant.

The agency may, on application by a social housing provider, investigate the circumstances of any tenant of social housing or an applicable person in relation to the tenant, to the extent that those circumstances might be relevant to the continued need of the tenant for the particular social housing that the tenant has been allocated, assigned, or let.

history.history-note: Section 121: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: Agency may ask questions

para.text: For the purpose of any investigation conducted under section 119, 120, or 121, the agency—
may ask any person whose circumstances it may investigate any relevant questions it thinks fit; and
may ask any person whose circumstances it may investigate to verify by statutory declaration—
any information he or she has given when answering questions asked under paragraph (a); or
any other information that he or she has at any time given to the agency; or
any information within his or her personal knowledge that has at any time been given to the agency by—
an applicable person in relation to that person (where the person whose circumstances are being investigated is a tenant); or
a person who would be an applicable person in relation to that person (where the person whose circumstances are being investigated is a prospective tenant); or
any person who was an applicable person in relation to the tenant or former tenant at the time concerned.

history.history-note: Section 122: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: Actions that may be taken by agency

para.text: The agency may take the actions stated in subsection (2) if—
any person whose circumstances it may investigate—
fails or refuses to answer (or, in the agency's opinion, fails or refuses to answer fully) any question asked under section 122(a); or
fails or refuses to verify any information by statutory declaration when asked to do so under section 122(b); or
it believes on reasonable grounds that any person whose circumstances it may investigate has deliberately given a false or misleading answer to any question asked under section 122(a); or
for the purpose of a review under section 117 or 118, the agency requires information from a tenant under section 125(1)﻿(d), and—
the tenant fails or refuses to comply fully with the requirement for information; or
the agency believes on reasonable grounds that the tenant has deliberately given false or misleading information in response to the requirement.
The actions are,—
to the extent that the tenant is the person or people to whom any social housing is let or to be let,—
calculate an income-related rent for the tenant on the basis of the agency's own understanding of the circumstances; or
calculate the income-related rent for the tenant as being equal to the market rent from time to time for the social housing:
to the extent that the tenant is a prospective tenant only,—
suspend the process of determining whether the tenant is eligible to be allocated social housing; or
suspend the process of determining whether the tenant is eligible to be referred or allocated to Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities or any registered community housing provider; or
decline the tenant's application to become a tenant of social housing:
to the extent that the tenant is an existing tenant only,—
treat the tenant as having had a change in circumstances (and the date of change in circumstances) on the basis of its own understanding of the circumstances; or
for the purpose of section 117, review, on the basis of the agency's own understanding of the circumstances, the eligibility of the tenant for social housing:
for the purpose of section 118, review, on the basis of the agency's own understanding of the circumstances, the tenant's housing needs:
treat the tenant as not eligible to be or continue to be allocated social housing, on the basis of the agency's own understanding of the circumstances.

history.history-note: Section 123: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).
Section 123(2)﻿(b)﻿(ii): amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).

prov.heading: Agency may seek information

para.text: For the purposes of a review under section 116, 117, or 118 or an investigation under section 119, 120, or 121, the agency may request any person to—
answer questions; or
allow the agency to inspect any document or other written information; or
give the agency—
a copy of any document or other written information; or
a printout of any information stored digitally.

The person does not have to comply with the request, but (for the purposes of section 24 of the Privacy Act 2020) this subsection authorises the person to make personal information available in response to the request.

history.history-note: Section 124: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).
Section 124(2): amended, on 1 December 2020, by section 217 of the Privacy Act 2020 (2020 No 31).

prov.heading: Agency may require information for certain purposes

para.text: The agency may by written notice require information from any person for any 1 or more of the following purposes:
the purpose of ascertaining the housing needs of a prospective tenant:
the purpose of ascertaining the eligibility of a tenant to be allocated social housing:
the purpose of calculating an appropriate income-related rent:
the purpose of a review under section 116, 117, or 118:
the purpose of any investigation under section 119, 120, or 121:
the purpose of detecting whether a person has committed or is committing an offence under section 130 or 131:
the purpose of determining and detecting the cost of fraud under section 130 or 131:
the purpose of ascertaining whether a person has failed or refused to answer fully, or has deliberately given a false or misleading answer, to any question asked under section 122(a).
The agency when requiring any information under subsection (1) must do so in accordance with the code of conduct.
The agency may in writing require any person to advise whether any information provided under subsection (1) is accurate.
A person from whom information is required under subsection (1) or advice is required under subsection (3) must comply with the requirement—
no later than 5 working days after the notice was given; and
in the manner specified in the notice, without charge to the agency.

This subsection authorises (for the purposes of section 24 of the Privacy Act 2020) any person who is required to provide information under subsection (1) or (3) to make personal information available in response to the requirement.
Subsection (1) does not—
require any person to provide any information or produce any document that would be privileged in a court of law:
require any person to provide any information or produce any document that is legally professionally privileged.
Subsection (6) does not apply to information that—
consists wholly or partly of, or relates wholly or partly to,—
the receipts, payments, income, expenditure, or financial transactions of a specified person (whether a lawyer, his or her client, or any other person); or

investment receipts (being receipts arising or accruing from any money lodged at any time with a lawyer for investment) of any person or persons (whether the lawyer, his or her clients, or any other person or persons); and
is contained in, or comprises the whole or part of, any book, account, statement, or other record prepared by or kept in connection with a trust account of the lawyer.
If a person refuses to disclose any information or document on the ground that it is privileged under subsection (6)﻿(a) or (b),—

the agency or that person or any other person to whom the information or document relates may apply to a District Court Judge for an order determining whether or not the claim of privilege is valid; and
for the purposes of determining that application, the Judge may require the information or document to be produced to the court.
In this section,—

lawyer means a barrister or solicitor of the High Court, and includes a firm, or an incorporated law firm (within the meaning of the Lawyers and Conveyancers Act 2006), in which the lawyer is, or is held out to be, a partner, director, or shareholder
trust account, in relation to a lawyer has the same meaning as in section 6 of the Lawyers and Conveyancers Act 2006.

history.history-note: Section 125: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).
Section 125(5): amended, on 1 December 2020, by section 217 of the Privacy Act 2020 (2020 No 31).

prov.heading: Powers of agency to use information

para.text: If the agency is the Ministry, the agency may, despite any other enactment,—
use information obtained under this Part and Schedule 4 to perform its functions, duties, and powers under the Social Security Act 2018; and
use information obtained under the Social Security Act 2018 to perform its functions, duties, and powers under this Part.

history.history-note: Section 126: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).
Section 126(a): amended, on 26 November 2018, by section 459 of the Social Security Act 2018 (2018 No 32).
Section 126(b): amended, on 26 November 2018, by section 459 of the Social Security Act 2018 (2018 No 32).

prov.heading: Recovery where rate of rent too low

para.text: Subsection (2) applies to a tenant of social housing and a period of time if, at any later time, the agency—
has in its possession information (whether or not obtained as a result of a review or an investigation under this Act) that—
it did not have during that period, or had but did not have reasonable grounds to believe; and
it now believes on reasonable grounds; and

is satisfied that, if it had had the information before the period and had had reasonable grounds to believe the information, it would have calculated for the tenant an income-related rent higher than the income-related rent that it last notified to the social housing provider in respect of the period (capped at the market rent).
If this subsection applies to a tenant of social housing and a period of time, the agency may calculate, and recover as a debt due to the Crown, the difference between—
the higher income-related rent it would have calculated for the tenant, but capped at the market rent for the housing, in respect of the period; and
the income-related rent calculated and notified for the tenant (capped at the market rent).
Amounts recoverable under subsection (2) are not rent in arrear for the purposes of the Residential Tenancies Act 1986.

history.history-note: Section 127: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: Recovery of debt where agency is Ministry

para.text: For the purpose of section 74(3), 94(2), or 127(2), if the agency is the Ministry,—
the agency must treat the debt as if it were a debt due to the Crown under regulations made under section 444 of the Social Security Act 2018, with any necessary modification; and
to avoid doubt, the agency may recover that debt in accordance with that Act from any tenant or former tenant of social housing.

history.history-note: Section 128: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).
Section 128(a): amended, on 26 November 2018, by section 459 of the Social Security Act 2018 (2018 No 32).
Heading: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: Allocation of social housing

para.text: The matters to which the agency may have regard in doing any of the things stated in subsection (2) may include criteria that have, or are capable of having, the effect that tenants, people who are or might be applicable persons in relation to those tenants, and other people who are or might be residing in the housing concerned are treated differently on the basis of—

their marital status, disability or absence of disability, age, or family status (as the terms marital status, disability, age, and family status are defined in section 21(1)﻿(b), (h), (i), and (l) of the Human Rights Act 1993); or
whether or not they are resident, or ordinarily resident, or permanently resident, or lawfully resident, in New Zealand; or
their incomes; or
their property; or
2 or more of those factors.
The things are any thing that the agency does in the course of determining eligibility for social housing or social housing offered by a social housing provider, and include—
assessing the housing needs of a tenant:
assessing the eligibility of prospective tenants to be allocated social housing:
reviewing the eligibility of tenants to be or continue to be allocated social housing, or reviewing the eligibility of a tenant under section 117 or the housing needs of a tenant under section 118:
operating a waiting list of tenants who have been assessed as being eligible for social housing, but who have yet to be allocated, assigned, or let social housing or particular social housing:
providing the results of its assessments of eligibility and housing needs to Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities or registered community housing providers:
referring or allocating prospective tenants to social housing providers.
Nothing in this section affects the application of the New Zealand Bill of Rights Act 1990.

history.history-note: Section 129: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).
Section 129(2)﻿(e): amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Heading: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: Offence not to provide information or to provide false or misleading information

para.text: A person who is required to provide information under section 125(1) commits an offence if the person—
fails or refuses to provide, without reasonable excuse, the information required:
provides false or misleading information in response to the requirement.
A person who is required under section 125(3) to advise whether the information provided under section 125(1) is accurate commits an offence if the person—
fails or refuses to provide that advice, without reasonable excuse:
provides false or misleading information in response to the requirement.
A person who commits an offence against this section is liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding $2,000.

history.history-note: Section 130: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: Offence to mislead agency for certain purposes or results

para.text: A person commits an offence who, for the purpose described in subsection (2) or with the result described in subsection (3),—
makes any statement knowing it to be false in any material particular; or
deliberately does or says anything for the purpose of misleading or attempting to mislead the agency; or

when required to advise the agency under section 115 or provide information under section 125, deliberately omits to do or say anything for the purpose of misleading or attempting to mislead the agency.
The purpose is—
for that person or another person to be eligible or continue to be eligible to be allocated social housing:

for that person or another person to be eligible or continue to be eligible to be allocated, assigned, or let particular social housing, or to be allocated, assigned, or let some other social housing:

for that person or another person to have calculated for them, or to pay or continue to pay as rent for social housing, an income-related rent or lower income-related rent than they would otherwise be entitled to under this Act or an income-related rent that they are not entitled to under this Act.
The result is that that person or another person, whether or not entitled to it under this Act,—
is or continues to be assessed as eligible to be allocated social housing:
is or continues to be allocated or assigned to a particular social housing provider:
is or continues to be allocated, assigned, or let particular social housing:
is allocated, assigned, or let some other social housing:
is let social housing at an income-related rent or lower income-related rent.
A person who commits an offence against this section is liable on conviction to imprisonment for a term not exceeding 12 months or a fine not exceeding $5,000, or both.

history.history-note: Section 131: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).
Heading: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: Rights of appeal

para.text: This section and sections 133 to 135 apply to—
any decision or determination of the agency made under this Part, the calculation mechanism, or Schedule 3 in respect of an income-related rent; and
any assessment by the agency of—
the eligibility of any tenant to be, or to continue to be, allocated social housing; or
the housing needs of any tenant.

Nothing in this section and sections 133 to 135, or in any regulations made under section 136, limits or affects the rights of any tenant of social housing, or of any social housing provider as landlord, under the Residential Tenancies Act 1986.

history.history-note: Section 132: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: Tenant may appeal

para.text: A tenant may, in accordance with regulations made under section 136, appeal against a decision, determination, or assessment referred to in section 132(1) if the agency—
has confirmed it (whether as originally made or as varied) under the process for the time being established by the agency to review such decisions, determinations, or assessments; or
has no such process for the time being established.

history.history-note: Section 133: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: Powers of appeal body

para.text: In determining the appeal, the appeal body has all the powers, duties, functions, and discretions the agency had in relation to the matter concerned and may—
confirm, modify, or reverse the decision, determination, or assessment; or
refer all or any part of the matter back to the agency for further consideration, together with—
any directions it thinks just relating to the reconsideration; and
a written statement of its reasons for doing so.
The appeal body—
may award costs against the agency in respect of any appeal if—
it is allowed in whole or in part; or
all or any part of the matter is referred back to the agency for further consideration; and
may award costs against the appellant in respect of any appeal that is refused, if the appeal body believes that it was frivolous or vexatious, or should not have been brought.

history.history-note: Section 134: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: Where appeal body established by regulations

para.text: If the agency is not the Ministry and regulations under section 136(1)﻿(a) establish a body to dispose of appeals under sections 132 to 134, any party to any appeal to the body who is dissatisfied with any determination of the body may appeal to the District Court and in that case,—
within 14 days after the date of the determination, the appellant must—
lodge a notice of appeal with the court; and
give a copy of the notice to every other party to the appeal:
the court or a Judge may, on the application of the appellant or intending appellant, extend any time prescribed or allowed under this section for the lodging of a notice of appeal:
except as provided by this subsection, the case must be dealt with in accordance with the rules of the District Court.

history.history-note: Section 135: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).
Section 135: amended, on 1 March 2017, by section 261 of the District Court Act 2016 (2016 No 49).
Heading: inserted, on 28 November 2013, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: Regulations relating to appeals and other matters

para.text: The Governor-General may, by Order in Council, make regulations for all or any of the following purposes:
for the purposes of section 133,—
establishing a body to dispose of appeals under that section, prescribing how its members are to be appointed, and prescribing how it is to hear and dispose of appeals; or

providing that such appeals are to be made to the social security appeal authority established by or under section 401 and Schedule 8 of the Social Security Act 2018 as if they were appeals against decisions or determinations under that Act, and providing that sections 395 to 410, regulations made under section 451, and Schedule 8 of that Act apply, with or without modification, to the hearing and disposal of appeals; or

providing, if the agency is not the Ministry, that such appeals are to be made to the District Court, and providing that the District Court Act 2016 and the rules of the District Court apply, with or without modification, to the hearing and disposal of appeals; or
providing for any other matters contemplated by the provisions of this Part (other than sections 107 to 113), necessary for their administration, or necessary for giving them full effect.
Any body established under subsection (1)﻿(a)﻿(i) is a statutory board within the meaning of the Fees and Travelling Allowances Act 1951, and every member of it is entitled to—
remuneration by way of fees, salary, or allowances for the member's services as a member of it:
payment of travelling allowances and expenses in respect of time spent travelling in its service.
If regulations made under subsection (1) provide for the matters described in subsection (1)﻿(a)﻿(ii) (relating to the Social Security Appeal Authority), regulations may—
provide for the function of the Benefits Review Committees in relation to appeals under this Act; and
require that an appeal to the Social Security Appeal Authority may not be heard until the matter has been confirmed or varied by the Benefits Review Committee.
Regulations under this section are secondary legislation (see Part 3 of the Legislation Act 2019 for publication requirements).

legtable.summary: The following table is small in size and has 3 columns that are grouped under the heading Legislation Act 2019 requirements for secondary legislation made under this section.

entry.emphasis: Legislation Act 2019 requirements for secondary legislation made under this section

row.entry: PCO must publish it on the legislation website and notify it in the Gazette
The Minister must present it to the House of Representatives
It may be disallowed by the House of Representatives

entry.emphasis: This note is not part of the Act.

history.history-note: Section 136: inserted, on 28 November 2013, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).
Section 136(1)﻿(a)﻿(ii): replaced, on 26 November 2018, by section 459 of the Social Security Act 2018 (2018 No 32).
Section 136(1)﻿(a)﻿(iii): amended, on 1 March 2017, by section 261 of the District Court Act 2016 (2016 No 49).
Section 136(4): inserted, on 28 October 2021, by section 3 of the Secondary Legislation Act 2021 (2021 No 7).
Heading: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: Delegation of powers under this Part

para.text: The agency—
may not delegate a power under section 116(2)﻿(b), 122(b), 123, or 127, except to a person who is an employee of the agency; and
may not delegate any other power under this Part, the calculation mechanism, or Schedule 3, except to a person who is—
an employee of the agency; or
engaged by the agency under a contract for services providing for the person to exercise that power.

Despite subsection (1), if the agency is a department, it may delegate any power under this Part to the chief executive of another department or Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities, or to an employee of the department, in accordance with clauses 2 and 3 of Schedule 6 of the Public Service Act 2020.
A power that has been delegated in accordance with subsection (2) may, with the consent of the agency, be delegated,—
by the chief executive of a department, to a departmental agency or an employee of that department or departmental agency:
by Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities, to an employee of Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities:
by an employee of the agency, to another employee of the agency.

If the agency delegates a power under this Part, the calculation mechanism, or Schedule 3 (not being a power referred to in subsection (1)﻿(a)) to a body corporate engaged by the agency under a contract for services,—
the body corporate cannot subdelegate it except to a person who is an employee of the body corporate; and
an employee of the body corporate to whom it is subdelegated cannot subdelegate it further.
This section overrides section 130(1) of the Companies Act 1993.

history.history-note: Section 137: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).
Section 137(2): amended, on 7 August 2020, by section 135 of the Public Service Act 2020 (2020 No 40).
Section 137(2): amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Section 137(3)﻿(b): replaced, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Section 137(3)﻿(c): repealed, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).
Heading: inserted, on 28 May 2015, by section 9 of the Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters (Social Housing Reform) Amendment Act 2015 (2015 No 50).

prov.label: 137A
prov.heading: Tailored agreements with social housing providers

para.text: The agency may, for the purposes of and in accordance with a direction under section 102(1A), enter into 1 or more agreements with a social housing provider for the provision, by that provider, of social housing and any related services, for the price and on the terms and conditions agreed between the agency and the provider.

history.history-note: Section 137A: inserted, on 28 May 2015, by section 9 of the Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters (Social Housing Reform) Amendment Act 2015 (2015 No 50).
Heading: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: Code of conduct applying to obtaining information under section 125

para.text: The agency, in consultation with the Privacy Commissioner must, within 3 months after the commencement of this section, issue a code of conduct that applies in respect of any requirement for information under section 125.
The code of conduct—
must include the matters specified in section 142; and
may include restrictions on obtaining—
specified classes of information; and
information from specified classes of persons or from persons in specified relationships; and
must specify procedures applying to the obtaining of information under section 125.
The agency may from time to time, in consultation with the Privacy Commissioner, amend the code of conduct, or revoke the code of conduct and issue a new code of conduct.

Nothing in the code of conduct may derogate from any code of practice issued by the Privacy Commissioner under section 32 of the Privacy Act 2020 that applies to the information required under section 125, and the agency, in consultation with the Privacy Commissioner, must amend the code of conduct to conform with any such code of practice. This subsection is affected by section 140.

As soon as practicable after issuing any code of conduct and any amendment to it under this section, the agency must arrange for it to be published on an Internet site that is publicly available at all reasonable times or published in a form that is otherwise accessible to the public.
The code of conduct that, before the commencement of this section, was issued under Part 5—
is, on the commencement of this section, deemed to be issued under this section; and
applies, subject to any necessary modification, in respect of any requirements for information under section 125.

history.history-note: Section 138: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).
Section 138(4): amended, on 1 December 2020, by section 217 of the Privacy Act 2020 (2020 No 31).

prov.heading: Who must comply with code of conduct

para.text: The following persons must comply with the code of conduct when requiring information under section 125:
the agency, the chief executive of the agency (if a department), and every employee of the agency:
every person to whom the power to require such information has been delegated under section 137:
every person or body corporate engaged by the agency under a contract for services providing for the person to exercise the power to require such information:
every employee of a person or body corporate referred to in paragraph (c).

history.history-note: Section 139: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: Regulations authorising information to be obtained

para.text: The Governor-General may, on the advice of the joint Ministers given after consultation with the Privacy Commissioner, by Order in Council, make regulations authorising the agency to obtain pursuant to a requirement under section 125—
any specified class of information; or
information from any specified class of persons; or
information in any specified manner.

Subsection (1) applies despite the fact that the making of that requirement under that section would otherwise be in breach of any code of practice issued by the Privacy Commissioner under section 32 of the Privacy Act 2020.
Regulations under this section are secondary legislation (see Part 3 of the Legislation Act 2019 for publication requirements).

legtable.summary: The following table is small in size and has 3 columns that are grouped under the heading Legislation Act 2019 requirements for secondary legislation made under this section.

entry.emphasis: Legislation Act 2019 requirements for secondary legislation made under this section

row.entry: PCO must publish it on the legislation website and notify it in the Gazette
The Minister must present it to the House of Representatives
It may be disallowed by the House of Representatives

entry.emphasis: This note is not part of the Act.

history.history-note: Section 140: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).
Section 140(2): amended, on 1 December 2020, by section 217 of the Privacy Act 2020 (2020 No 31).
Section 140(3): inserted, on 28 October 2021, by section 3 of the Secondary Legislation Act 2021 (2021 No 7).

prov.heading: Complaints

para.text: Any person who is required to provide any information under section 125 or who is the subject of that information may make a complaint to the Privacy Commissioner that the requirement breaches the code of conduct.
Parts 5 and 6 of the Privacy Act 2020 apply to the complaint as if the code of conduct were a code of practice issued under section 32 of that Act.

history.history-note: Section 141: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).
Section 141(2): replaced, on 1 December 2020, by section 217 of the Privacy Act 2020 (2020 No 31).

prov.heading: Matters to be included in code of conduct

para.text: The code of conduct issued under section 138 must contain the following matters:
provisions requiring any information to be first sought, as the case may require, except where compliance with the provision would prejudice the maintenance of the law, from—
the tenant or an applicable person in relation to the tenant; or
the prospective tenant or the person who would be an applicable person in relation to the prospective tenant; or
a former tenant:

provisions allowing a person referred to in paragraph (a) the time that is specified in the code to provide the information before the agency requires that information, or advice on the accuracy of that information, from another person or agency, except where compliance with such provision would prejudice the maintenance of the law:

a provision prohibiting a requirement under section 125 being made in respect of a person referred to in paragraph (d) (not being a lawyer, an employer, or a former employer of a person referred to in paragraph (a) and not being a department, departmental agency, Crown entity, financial institution), unless there is reasonable cause to make a requirement under that section:

a provision prohibiting a requirement under section 125 being made to an employer in respect of any information that relates solely to the marital or relationship status of an employee or former employee of that employer:

provisions otherwise restricting requirements under section 125 being made to an employer to information specified in the code relating to the employment and the address of an employee or former employee of that employer.
In subsection (1)﻿(c), reasonable cause includes—
cause to suspect that the person—
has committed an offence under this Act; or
has obtained by fraud any income-related rent or social housing; or
as a result of committing fraud, pays or continues to pay an income-related rent or remains in social housing or particular social housing:

the fact that the person has failed, within the time specified in the code, or has refused to provide information in accordance with a requirement made to that person under a provision referred to in subsection (1)﻿(a).

history.history-note: Section 142: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

part.crosshead: Debt recovery by agency other than Ministry

history.history-note: Heading: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: Debt recovery by agency other than Ministry

para.text: If the agency is not the Ministry of Social Development, subsection (2) applies to the exercise by the agency of its power to recover debt under section 74(3), 94(2), or 127.
The agency may not recover any sum comprising that part of the debt that was caused wholly or partly by an error to which the tenant did not intentionally contribute if—
the tenant acted in good faith in—
continuing to pay the lower income-related rent during the period to which the debt relates; or
failing to advise the agency in accordance with section 115(1); or
it would be inequitable in all the circumstances, including the tenant's financial circumstances, to permit recovery.
In subsection (2), error means—
any incorrect information provided to or held by the agency:
any erroneous act or omission of the agency that occurs during an investigation or review under this Part:
any other erroneous act or omission by the agency.

history.history-note: Section 143: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: Application of sections 145 to 155

para.text: Sections 145 to 155 apply for the purpose of debt recovery under section 74(3), 94(2), or 127 to the agency only if the agency is not the Ministry.

history.history-note: Section 144: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).
Heading: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: Interpretation of deduction notice sections of this Part

para.text: In this section and in sections 146 to 155, unless the context otherwise requires,—
deduction notice means a notice issued under section 146
overdue amount means the amount—
recoverable as a debt due to the Crown under section 74(3), 94(2), or 127(2); and
that remains unpaid and includes any part of any amount of that kind
payment, in relation to a third party, includes payments made, or to be made, by the third party as—
salary or wages:
a retiring allowance or pension or other payment of a similar nature:
a benefit:
weekly compensation under the Accident Compensation Act 2001:
a bonus or an incentive payment:
a commission:
consideration for work performed under a contract for services
tenant—
means 1 or more people to whom social housing is or was let at an income-related rent; and
includes any former tenant
third party means the person required to make 1 or more deductions under a deduction notice.

history.history-note: Section 145: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: Deduction of overdue Crown debt

para.text: The agency may recover an amount as a debt due to the Crown by issuing a deduction notice in accordance with this section if any overdue amount remains unpaid, after the agency has reminded the tenant of the obligation to pay the amount and has done all it reasonably can to obtain repayment of the unpaid amount.
Nothing in subsection (1) requires the chief executive to take proceedings in any court to establish or recover the debt before issuing a deduction notice.

The deduction notice may require a third party to deduct an amount specified in the notice due from any payment that is payable or will become payable by the third party to the tenant, whether that payment will be made—
on the third party's own account; or
in the third party's capacity as an agent or a trustee; or
for any other reason.

history.history-note: Section 146: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: Matters relating to deduction notice

para.text: The agency must specify in the deduction notice—
whether the deduction is to be made as a lump sum or by instalments; and
the time or times by which the amounts deducted must be paid to the agency; and
the date on which the deduction notice takes effect, being a date not earlier than 7 working days after the date on which it was issued.
The agency must make all reasonable efforts to give the tenant a copy of a deduction notice within 7 working days after the notice is issued.
A deduction notice is revoked when the agency notifies the third party in writing to that effect or issues a new deduction notice to that third party.
The agency—
may revoke a deduction notice at any time:
must revoke the deduction notice if satisfied that the overdue amount has been paid.
Every deduction notice is subject to sections 148 to 155.
A notice under this section may be given by electronic means in accordance with subpart 2 of Part 4 of the Contract and Commercial Law Act 2017.

history.history-note: Section 147: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).
Section 147(6): amended, on 1 September 2017, by section 347 of the Contract and Commercial Law Act 2017 (2017 No 5).

prov.heading: Issue of deduction notice to State sector employer

para.text: In any case where a tenant is employed within a department (as defined in section 5 of the Public Service Act 2020), a deduction notice may be issued under section 146 to the chief executive of that department in respect of any salary or wages payable to the tenant.

history.history-note: Section 148: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).
Section 148: amended, on 7 August 2020, by section 135 of the Public Service Act 2020 (2020 No 40).

prov.heading: Discharge of debt

para.text: In any case where a third party deducts, under a deduction notice, any money payable to a tenant, the tenant is, to the extent of the amount deducted, discharged from his or her debt to the Crown.

history.history-note: Section 149: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: Deduction notices issued to banks

para.text: Where the third party is a bank, any money held by the bank to the credit of the tenant is subject to the provisions of section 146 and the amount required to be deducted under the deduction notice is, without prejudice to any other remedies against the tenant or any other person, deemed to be held in trust for the Crown and is a debt due to the Crown and may be recovered by the agency in any court or tribunal of competent jurisdiction.

For the purposes of this section, bank means a person carrying on in New Zealand the business of banking, a credit union within the meaning of the Friendly Societies and Credit Unions Act 1982, and a building society within the meaning of the Building Societies Act 1965, but does not include the Reserve Bank of New Zealand continued under the Reserve Bank of New Zealand Act 2021 (except in relation to an account maintained by that bank for an employee of the bank).

For the purposes of this section, money held by the bank to the credit of the tenant includes money, and any interest on money, that is on deposit or deposited with a bank to the credit of the tenant, whether or not—
the deposit or depositing is on current account:
the money is to be at interest at a fixed term or without limitation of time:
the tenant has made any application to withdraw or uplift the money.
For the purposes of this section, money on deposit with a bank is deemed to be to the credit of the tenant if the money—
is held in a joint bank account in the name of the tenant and 1 or more other persons; and
can be withdrawn from the account by or on behalf of the tenant without a signature being required at the time of that withdrawal from, or on behalf of, the other person or persons.

history.history-note: Section 150: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).
Section 150(2): amended, on 1 July 2022, by section 300(1) of the Reserve Bank of New Zealand Act 2021 (2021 No 31).

prov.heading: Making of deductions

para.text: Any person who makes a deduction under a deduction notice is deemed to be acting—

on the authority of the tenant and any other person concerned, and neither the tenant nor that other person has any claim against the third party or the agency or the Crown in respect of that deduction; and

on behalf of the agency, and, without prejudice to any other remedies against the tenant or any other person, any amount deducted must be held in trust for the Crown and is a debt due to the Crown and may be recovered by the agency in any court or tribunal of competent jurisdiction.
A third party must, on request, give the tenant a statement in writing of any amount deducted, and of the purpose for which the deduction was made.

history.history-note: Section 151: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: Offences in relation to deduction notices

para.text: Every person commits an offence and is liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding $2,000 who—
fails to make any deduction required by a deduction notice; or
fails, after making a deduction, to pay the amount deducted to the agency within the time specified in the notice; or
permits payment to or on behalf of any person, other than the agency, of any amount deemed to be held in trust for the Crown under sections 150 and 151.

Every employer commits an offence and is liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding $1,000 who dismisses an employee or alters an employee's position in the employer's business or undertaking to the employee's prejudice by reason of a deduction notice having been issued to the employer.

history.history-note: Section 152: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: Protected earnings

para.text: Despite anything in sections 146 to 151, where a deduction notice is issued to an employer of a tenant, the employer must not, in making deductions under the deduction notice, reduce the amount paid to the tenant by way of salary or wages in respect of any week to an amount that is less than 60% of the amount calculated as being the tenant's net ordinary weekly pay for a week.

For the purposes of this section, the tenant's net ordinary weekly pay for a week is the balance left after deducting from the tenant's ordinary weekly pay (as defined in section 8 of the Holidays Act 2003) the amount of tax required to be withheld or deducted in accordance with the PAYE rules of the Income Tax Act 2007 if that ordinary weekly pay were the only salary or wages paid to the tenant by the employer in respect of a week.

history.history-note: Section 153: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: Penalty for late deductions

para.text: A third party is liable to pay to the agency a penalty calculated in accordance with subsection (2) if the third party fails wholly or in part to—
deduct the amount required by the notice; or
pay any amount deducted under the notice to the agency by the time specified in the notice.
The penalty referred to in subsection (1) must be calculated as follows:
on the amount in default, 10% of that amount or $5, whichever is the greater:

for each additional month or part of a month in which the amount in default or any part of the amount has not been deducted or, as the case may be, has not been paid to the agency, a further penalty of 2% of that amount or part of the amount or $1, whichever is the greater.

The agency may, in its discretion, remit the whole or part of a penalty if satisfied that the failure to make the deduction or the payment was due to circumstances reasonably beyond the third party's control, or that, in all the circumstances, the imposition of that penalty would be inequitable.
If the agency decides to remit the whole or part of any penalty and any amount of the penalty has been paid under this section, the agency may refund any excess.
An amount payable to the agency under subsection (1) is a debt due to the Crown and may be recovered by the agency in any court or tribunal of competent jurisdiction.

history.history-note: Section 154: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: How notice may be given

para.text: Every notice given to any person under any of sections 146 to 154 may be given by delivering it to that person,—
in the case of a natural person (other than an officer or employee in the service of the Crown in his or her official capacity),—
personally; or
by leaving it at that person's usual or last known place of residence or business or at the address specified by that person in any application or other document received from that person; or
by posting it in a letter addressed to that person at that place of residence or business or at that address:
in the case of any other person, including an officer or employee in the service of the Crown in his or her official capacity,—
where applicable, personally; or
by leaving it at that person's place of business; or
by posting it in a letter addressed to that person at that place of business.

If any such notice is sent to any person by post, then, in the absence of evidence to the contrary, the notice is deemed to have been received by that person on the fourth day after the day on which it is posted, and, in proving the delivery, it is sufficient to prove the letter was properly addressed and posted.
A notice to a person under any of sections 146 to 154 may be given by electronic means in accordance with subpart 2 of Part 4 of the Contract and Commercial Law Act 2017.

history.history-note: Section 155: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).
Section 155(3): amended, on 1 September 2017, by section 347 of the Contract and Commercial Law Act 2017 (2017 No 5).
Heading: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).

prov.heading: Transitional arrangements for certain tenants

para.text: This section and Schedule 3 apply to a tenant of Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities if, but for the provisions of that schedule,—
the rent that the tenant would be required to pay for the first rent period commencing on or after the appointed day—
would be greater than—

the rent, after the deduction of any accommodation assistance (within the meaning of clause 1 of Schedule 3) to which the tenant was entitled, that the tenant was required to pay for the last rent period commencing before the appointed day.
Schedule 3 overrides section 104.

history.history-note: Section 156: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).
Section 156(1): amended, on 1 October 2019, by section 32 of the Kāinga Ora–Homes and Communities Act 2019 (2019 No 50).

prov.heading: Responsible department may verify entitlement

para.text: For the purposes of section 156 and Schedule 3,—

the agency may, with the consent of the applicable persons concerned, give the department that is, with the authority of the Prime Minister, for the time being responsible for the administration of the Social Security Act 2018 details of—
those persons' assessable incomes and the components of those incomes, and their cash assets, as supplied—
to the agency for the purpose of section 104(1); and
the weekly market rent in relation to the housing; and
the dates of the beginning and end of the rent period in respect of the housing that commenced immediately prior to the appointed day; and
an officer or employee of the department may, on the basis of the details given under paragraph (a),—

certify in writing to the applicable persons the kinds of accommodation assistance (within the meaning of clause 1 of Schedule 3) they were receiving and, in respect of assistance of each kind, whether they were receiving more than their entitlement, their entitlement, or less than their entitlement immediately before the appointed day; and
give the agency a copy of the certificate; and
the agency may rely on the certificate.

history.history-note: Section 157: inserted, on 14 April 2014, by section 21 of the Social Housing Reform (Housing Restructuring and Tenancy Matters Amendment) Act 2013 (2013 No 97).
